dimensions.dimension: A (infra): object creation/lifetime + reusable parent-child tree (A1, A2, C5)
dimensions.summary: This engine is a hand-rolled UObject/GC clone of UE. Objects are created via UObjectManager::Get().CreateObject<T>(Outer) (there is NO NewObject<> — that token only appears once in a property helper), and destroyed via a two-phase deferred GC (DestroyObject -> BeginDestroy + RF_PendingKill, then a mark/sweep that purges). Liveness is tracked through a global GUObjectSet and per-object SerialNumber; the discipline a UI tree must follow is: a GC root (a Singleton FGCObject or a rooted UObject) must own the tree and report every node from AddReferencedObjects(), otherwise nodes get swept. There IS an existing, battle-tested N-level parent/child tree to mirror: USceneComponent's attachment hierarchy (ParentComponent: TWeakObjectPtr + ChildComponents: TArray, with SetParent/AddChild/RemoveChild/GetChildren/IsDescendantOf and cycle rejection). It also already demonstrates the exact pattern the design needs: a parent-relative transform cached lazily and propagated to children via a dirty-flag (MarkTransformDirty recurses into ChildComponents) — directly analogous to GlobalScale/RectTransform recomputation. For C5, USceneComponent fuses transform onto the node (logic lives in subclasses/sibling UPrimitiveComponent, transform lives on USceneComponent itself); it does NOT use a separate Slot object. So the recommendation is to put RectTransform as a value-struct member ON the Element node, mirroring how FTransform RelativeTransform is a member of USceneComponent — a separate Slot/Transform object would be over-engineering inconsistent with this engine's patterns. The new model should be a SEPARATE lightweight UObject tree (Canvas/Group/Element : UObject), NOT built on the RmlUi UUserWidget path (which wraps Rml::ElementDocument HTML/CSS and has no pivot/anchor/px-rect concept), though it should reuse the same GC-keepalive idiom UUIManager already uses (UUIManager is an FGCObject that keeps widgets alive via AddReferencedObjects).

findings.conclusion: Objects derive from UObject and are created with UObjectManager::Get().CreateObject<T>(InOuter), which news the T, sets its Outer (logical scope, NOT ownership), and auto-names it. There is NO NewObject<> free function in this engine (the only 'NewObject' grep hit is an unrelated identifier in ObjectProperty.cpp); CreateObject<> is the real factory, used in 30+ files. The Canvas/Group/Element nodes should be UObjects created the same way.

evidence.note: UObjectManager::CreateObject<T>(UObject* InOuter): static_assert derives-from-UObject, new T(), SetOuter, auto-name via NameCounters
Outer is 'logical scope (not ownership)', excluded from serialization — so Outer is NOT what keeps a child alive
only file matching 'NewObject' — confirms no NewObject<> factory exists; CreateObject<> is the idiom

findings.conclusion: Lifetime is a two-phase deferred GC, NOT immediate delete or refcounting. DestroyObject() only runs BeginDestroy()+MarkPendingKill() and requests a collection; actual delete happens in FGarbageCollector sweep. Reachability is computed by walking AddReferencedObjects() from roots (RF_RootSet UObjects + registered FGCObject external roots). DISCIPLINE FOR UI TREE: every node must be reachable from a root each GC pass or it is purged. The practical pattern is a Singleton manager that is an FGCObject and reports the root(s) in AddReferencedObjects; children are kept alive by their parent's AddReferencedObjects (as AActor does for components).

evidence.note: DestroyObject: BeginDestroy + MarkPendingKill + RequestGarbageCollection; no synchronous free. DestroyObjectImmediate just forwards to DestroyObject
FGCObject: abstract external root; subclass overrides AddReferencedObjects + GetReferencerName to keep objects alive
MarkObject / AddExternalRoot / RemoveExternalRoot — how a manager registers as a GC root

findings.conclusion: Three smart-pointer flavors exist. TObjectPtr<T> = thin owning-intent raw wrapper (used in UPROPERTY(Instanced) lists; it is what AddReferencedObjects walks to keep things alive). TWeakObjectPtr<T> = serial-number-validated non-owning back-reference (rejects PendingKill/Garbage). TSubclassOf<T> = type-checked UClass* for 'spawn of class X'. UI tree should use: TObjectPtr (or TArray<TObjectPtr>) reported via AddReferencedObjects for parent->child (owning, keepalive), and TWeakObjectPtr for child->parent (back-ref, no keepalive, no cycle).

evidence.note: TObjectPtr<T>: thin raw-pointer wrapper, GetValid() filters via ::IsValid; this is the owning-edge type
TWeakObjectPtr::Get() validates SerialNumber + rejects RF_PendingKill|RF_Garbage — safe back-reference, does not keep alive
TSubclassOf<T>: type-guarded UClass* slot, used for class-typed spawn fields

findings.conclusion: No pools/arenas/freelists for UObjects: operator new/delete just std::malloc/free with MemoryStats accounting. So the UI tree gets no special allocator benefit from being UObjects; the only reason to make nodes UObjects is GC + reflection/serialization + the existing TObjectPtr/WeakObjectPtr machinery.

evidence.note: UObject::operator new/delete = std::malloc/std::free + MemoryStats — no pool/arena/freelist

findings.conclusion: YES — USceneComponent is a ready-made N-level parent/child node tree the Canvas->Group->Element tree should mirror almost 1:1. Exact API: add child = AddChild(child) (forwards to child->SetParent(this)) or AttachToComponent(parent, socket); set/change parent = SetParent(newParent); iterate children = GetChildren() (const TArray<USceneComponent*>&) or GetChildComponents() (copy); get parent = GetParent(). It already enforces no-cycle (SetParent rejects if newParent IsDescendantOf this) and self-attach. Storage: ChildComponents = TArray<USceneComponent*> (raw, parent->child), ParentComponent = TWeakObjectPtr<USceneComponent> (child->parent back-ref). This is exactly the owning-list + weak-backref shape recommended in A1.

evidence.note: Hierarchy API: AttachToComponent/SetParent/GetParent/AddChild/RemoveChild/ContainsChild/IsDescendantOf/GetChildComponents/GetChildren
ParentComponent = TWeakObjectPtr<USceneComponent>; ChildComponents = TArray<USceneComponent*> — the storage to mirror
SetParent: cycle check (IsDescendantOf), detach from old parent, push_back into new parent's ChildComponents, MarkTransformDirty; AddChild forwards to child->SetParent(this)

findings.conclusion: AActor is the other tree shape but is the WRONG mirror for Canvas->Group->Element: it is a flat owner of a component LIST (OwnedComponents: TArray<TObjectPtr<UActorComponent>> + a single RootComponent), not a recursive node. It does, however, demonstrate the GC-keepalive contract precisely: AActor::AddReferencedObjects reports RootComponent + OwnedComponents so the whole component set survives sweep. The Canvas (root) should likewise report its child Groups, and each node its children, from AddReferencedObjects.

evidence.note: RootComponent: TObjectPtr<USceneComponent> (Transient,Instanced) + OwnedComponents: TArray<TObjectPtr<UActorComponent>> — flat list, not recursive
AActor::AddReferencedObjects reports RootComponent + OwnedComponents to keep the tree alive through GC — the exact pattern UI nodes must follow
USceneComponent::AddReferencedObjects reports ChildComponents — recursive keepalive already implemented per-node; copy this for Element->children

findings.conclusion: The existing RmlUi widget tree (UUserWidget/UUIManager) is NOT a usable parent/child node tree for this model. UUserWidget is a flat wrapper around a single Rml::ElementDocument (HTML/CSS); the actual element tree lives inside RmlUi (Rml::Element), addressed by string IDs via SetText/SetProperty — there is no pivot/anchor/px-RectTransform concept and no UObject-level child list. UUIManager just holds flat TArrays of widgets. So the new simple model should be a SEPARATE lightweight UObject tree, reusing the SceneComponent hierarchy pattern, not built on RmlUi.

evidence.note: UUserWidget wraps one Rml::ElementDocument*; element access is by string id (SetText/SetProperty) — no rect/anchor/pivot, no UObject child tree
UUIManager is a Singleton+FGCObject holding flat TArray<UUserWidget*> lists; RmlUi (Rml::Context) does the real layout via CSS
CreateWidget uses CreateObject<UUserWidget>(); UUIManager::AddReferencedObjects keeps widgets alive — reuse this FGCObject manager idiom for the new Canvas root

findings.conclusion: Put RectTransform ON the Element node as a value-struct member (FUIRectTransform RectTransform { pivot, anchor, position, size }), mirroring how USceneComponent stores FTransform RelativeTransform directly as a member rather than in a separate Slot object. This engine's precedent FUSES coords onto the node: USceneComponent IS the transform node, and differing logic is expressed by subclassing/sibling components, not by a Slot. Crucially, SceneComponent also already gives the lazy-cache + dirty-propagation recipe the layout math needs: a cached world matrix recomputed on demand and a MarkTransformDirty that recurses into all children — the Element should cache its computed FinalPos/scaled rect and invalidate children the same way when its RectTransform or the Canvas GlobalScale changes. A separate Slot/Transform object (UE FSlateChildSize / UMG Slot style) would be over-engineering against this codebase's grain and is explicitly the kind of Unreal-copying the brief warns against.

evidence.note: FTransform RelativeTransform is a direct member of USceneComponent (with per-field UPROPERTY) — transform fused onto the node, no separate Slot object. Mirror this with FUIRectTransform on Element
MarkTransformDirty: early-out if already dirty, then recurse into every Child — exact dirty-propagation pattern for GlobalScale/RectTransform recompute
UpdateWorldMatrix: child world = relativeMatrix * parent world, computed lazily on dirty — analogous to FinalPos = ParentOrigin + AnchorPx + position - size*pivot composed down the tree

openQuestions: Does the engine's reflection/serialization (UPROPERTY Save, .Scene save path) need the UI tree to be serializable, or is the in-game editor purely runtime? SceneComponent excludes Parent/Child from serialization and rebuilds topology explicitly (OnPostLoad note at SceneComponent.cpp:163-170) — the UI model would need the same explicit topology-rebuild if it is to be saved.

Who owns/roots the Canvas at runtime? Options seen: (a) a new Singleton FGCObject manager like UUIManager, (b) attach under a PlayerController/HUD, or (c) AddToRoot the Canvas. This choice determines the GC keepalive wiring and was not fully resolved here.

Should the UI node tree literally subclass USceneComponent (reusing SetParent/AddChild/GetChildren/IsDescendantOf for free, ignoring the 3D FTransform), or be a parallel minimal UObject tree that copies just the hierarchy methods? Subclassing reuses cycle-checking + GC code but drags in unused 3D transform/socket machinery.

verdicts.claim: [A1] Lifetime is a two-phase deferred GC: DestroyObject runs BeginDestroy()+MarkPendingKill()+RequestGarbageCollection() with no synchronous free; DestroyObjectImmediate forwards to DestroyObject. FGCObject is an abstract external root with AddReferencedObjects/GetReferencerName. Reachability walks AddReferencedObjects from roots (RF_RootSet UObjects + registered FGCObjects); children kept alive by parent's AddReferencedObjects as AActor does for components.
verdicts.note: Object.h:266-285 confirms DestroyObject does BeginDestroy()+MarkPendingKill()+RequestGarbageCollection() with no delete; DestroyObjectImmediate (289-292) forwards to DestroyObject. GarbageCollection.h:79-92 confirms FGCObject abstract (pure virtual AddReferencedObjects) + GetReferencerName. Lines 108-110 confirm MarkObject/AddExternalRoot/RemoveExternalRoot. GarbageCollection.cpp:213-256 MarkRoots confirms it iterates GUObjectArray for IsRooted()==RF_RootSet roots AND walks ExternalRoots->AddReferencedObjects. AActor.cpp:874-880 confirms AActor::AddReferencedObjects keeps RootComponent+OwnedComponents alive (AActor.h:197 comment explicitly says so). Minor caveat: MarkObject(line 108) marks an object reachable, not strictly 'registers a manager as root' (that is AddExternalRoot), but all three exist as cited.

verdicts.claim: [A2] USceneComponent is an N-level parent/child tree with AddChild (forwards to child->SetParent(this)), AttachToComponent, SetParent, GetChildren()/GetChildComponents(), GetParent(); enforces no-cycle via IsDescendantOf and self-attach rejection; storage ChildComponents=TArray<USceneComponent*> (raw owning), ParentComponent=TWeakObjectPtr<USceneComponent> (weak back-ref); SetParent detaches old parent, push_back into new parent, MarkTransformDirty.
verdicts.note: SceneComponent.h:26-45 confirms full hierarchy API including AddChild/RemoveChild/ContainsChild/IsDescendantOf/GetChildComponents()(copy)/GetChildren()(const ref). Lines 145-146 confirm ParentComponent=TWeakObjectPtr<USceneComponent>, ChildComponents=TArray<USceneComponent*>. SceneComponent.cpp:249-286 confirms SetParent: self-check (NewParent==this) and cycle check (NewParent->IsDescendantOf(this)) reject, OldParent->RemoveChild(this), ParentComponent.Reset(NewParent), Parent->ChildComponents.push_back(this), MarkTransformDirty(); AddChild(278-286) forwards to NewChild->SetParent(this). All specifics verified.

verdicts.claim: [C5] RectTransform should be a value-struct member on Element mirroring USceneComponent storing FTransform RelativeTransform as a direct member (per-field UPROPERTY) rather than a separate Slot; MarkTransformDirty early-outs if already dirty then recurses into every child; UpdateWorldMatrix computes child world = relativeMatrix * parent world lazily on dirty.
verdicts.note: SceneComponent.h:152-154 confirms FTransform RelativeTransform is a direct member with per-field UPROPERTY (Location line 152, Scale line 153, Rotation line 155) — transform fused onto node, no Slot. SceneComponent.cpp:543-557 confirms MarkTransformDirty early-outs (if bTransformDirty && bInverseWorldDirty return) then loops all ChildComponents calling Child->MarkTransformDirty() — exact dirty-propagation recursion. SceneComponent.cpp:346-409 confirms UpdateWorldMatrix early-outs unless bTransformDirty, computes CachedWorldMatrix = RelativeMatrix * Parent->GetWorldMatrix() (lines 387/399) lazily, sets bTransformDirty=false. Architectural/recommendation framing is opinion but the cited engine-precedent facts are accurate.

dimensions.dimension: A3 + C1: per-frame loop entry points, tick ordering, and UI Layout Pass insertion candidates
dimensions.summary: The per-frame chain is fully synchronous and single-threaded on the game thread: Launch -> FEngineLoop::Run loop -> GEngine->Tick (UGameEngine::Tick) -> UEngine::TickFrameStart (watcher/notifications/input poll) then UEngine::TickFrameBody, which runs Audio->WorldTick->Render IN THAT ORDER. WorldTick iterates every FWorldContext and calls UWorld::Tick, which dispatches actor & component ticks through FTickManager in 4 fixed TickGroups (PrePhysics, DuringPhysics[+physics submit], PostPhysics, PostUpdateWork) then TickPlayerCamera. Render() then runs the render pipeline, and the existing RmlUi UI is NOT ticked anywhere in the game-tick path — its only per-frame work (RmlContext->Update() + Render()) is driven from INSIDE the render pass FUIPass::Execute -> UUIManager::Render (UIManager.cpp:831/833), i.e. UI "layout/update" is currently conflated with UI rendering and happens after the whole 3D pass chain, late in the frame. For C1, the cleanest insertion point for a NEW simple UI Layout Pass is a dedicated step in UEngine::TickFrameBody AFTER WorldTick(DeltaTime) and BEFORE Render(DeltaTime) (Engine.cpp:174-179) — game-thread, post-gameplay, pre-submission. A second viable hook is at the end of UWorld::Tick. Folding it into the render pass (mirroring the RmlUi pattern at UIPass.cpp:27) is possible but mixes layout with GPU submission and is the weaker option.

findings.conclusion: Per-frame loop entry: WinMain-style Launch() calls GuardedMain which constructs FEngineLoop and calls EngineLoop.Run(). Run() is the frame loop: while(!exit){ PumpMessages; Timer.Tick(); GEngine->Tick(dt); }. GEngine is the concrete UGameEngine (standalone) / UEditorEngine / UObjViewerEngine, chosen by the CreateConcreteEngine factory injected from Launch.cpp.

evidence.note: GuardedMain: const int ExitCode = EngineLoop.Run();
FEngineLoop::Run() frame loop: PumpMessages, Timer.Tick(), GEngine->Tick(Timer.GetDeltaTime()) at line 72
CreateConcreteEngine factory picks UGameEngine (WITH_STANDALONE) / UEditorEngine / UObjViewerEngine

findings.conclusion: GEngine->Tick resolves to UGameEngine::Tick: TickFrameStart(dt) -> input snapshot + viewport ProcessInput + player input + ESC -> TickFrameBody(dt) -> ProcessPendingTransition. The base UEngine::Tick is simpler (TickFrameStart + TickFrameBody only). The shared body is UEngine::TickFrameBody which runs, strictly in order: FAudioManager::Tick(), WorldTick(dt), Render(dt). This ordering (gameplay world tick, THEN render) is the key fact for inserting a layout pass.

evidence.note: UGameEngine::Tick: TickFrameStart, viewport ProcessInput, TickFrameBody(dt) at line 88, ProcessPendingTransition
UEngine::TickFrameBody: Audio Tick; WorldTick(DeltaTime); Render(DeltaTime) in this exact order
Base UEngine::Tick = TickFrameStart + TickFrameBody

findings.conclusion: Actor/component Tick ordering: UEngine::WorldTick loops every FWorldContext and calls UWorld::Tick(dt, TickType). UWorld::Tick gathers tick functions (FTickManager::GatherTickFunctions queues each actor's PrimaryActorTick then iterates its components queuing PrimaryComponentTick), then runs FOUR ordered TickGroups: TG_PrePhysics -> physics submit -> TG_DuringPhysics -> physics wait/apply -> TG_PostPhysics -> TG_PostUpdateWork, finishing with TickPlayerCamera(). Within a group, order is the gather order: for each actor, the actor tick is queued before its components. ExecuteTick dispatches to AActor::TickActor and UActorComponent::TickComponent.

evidence.note: UEngine::WorldTick: loops WorldList, ToLevelTickType, World->Tick(DeltaTime, TickType) at line 269
UWorld::Tick: GatherTickFunctions then TickGroup(TG_PrePhysics/DuringPhysics/PostPhysics/PostUpdateWork) then TickPlayerCamera
GatherTickFunctions: per actor queue PrimaryActorTick (105) then each component's PrimaryComponentTick (114)
FActorComponentTickFunction::ExecuteTick -> Target->TickComponent(DeltaTime, TickType, *this)

findings.conclusion: The existing RmlUi UIManager is NOT ticked relative to the game tick at all. There is no UUIManager::Get().Tick()/Update() call anywhere in Runtime (grep for UIManager::Get() in Runtime returns only Initialize at Engine.cpp:102 and Shutdown at Engine.cpp:120). The only per-frame UI work — RmlContext->Update() and RmlContext->Render() — runs INSIDE the render pass chain, in FUIPass::Execute -> UUIManager::Render. So UI update happens AFTER WorldTick and AFTER all 3D passes, late in Render(), and layout is conflated with rendering. UI also pulls input directly inside Render via UUIManager::ProcessInput(Frame), not from the game-thread input stage.

evidence.note: FUIPass::Execute calls UUIManager::Get().Render(Ctx) — the ONLY per-frame UI driver
UUIManager::Render: SetDimensions, ProcessInput(Frame) at 824, RmlContext->Update() at 831, RmlContext->Render() at 833 — update+render together in the render pass
Only Runtime references to UIManager are Initialize (102) and Shutdown (120); no per-frame Tick

findings.conclusion: UIPass actually executes within the game render path: UEngine::Render -> RenderPipeline->Execute (FGameRenderPipeline::Execute) -> Renderer.Render(Frame, World, Scene) -> FRenderPassPipeline::Execute, which loops all registered passes in ERenderPass enum order calling BeginPass/Execute/EndPass. UI is the second-to-last pass (after OverlayFont, before GammaCorrection) per RenderPassMap, so it draws on top of the 3D scene into the viewport RTV. FUIPass::BeginPass gates on Ctx.Frame.ViewportRTV && UUIManager::HasViewportWidgets(), so with zero widgets the pass is skipped entirely.

evidence.note: FGameRenderPipeline::Execute: BuildFrame, CollectCommands, Renderer.Render(Frame, World, *Scene) at 77, then Blit/EndFrame
FRenderer::Render builds FPassContext and calls Pipeline.Execute(PassCtx)
FRenderPassPipeline::Execute loops Passes in order calling BeginPass/Execute/EndPass
Pass order: OverlayFont, UI, GammaCorrection — UI is the last content pass before gamma

findings.conclusion: PRIMARY candidate insertion point for a NEW simple UI Layout Pass: a dedicated call inside UEngine::TickFrameBody between WorldTick(DeltaTime) and Render(DeltaTime) at Engine.cpp:177-178. This runs on the game thread, after all gameplay/actor/component ticks have settled transforms and game state, and before any render submission — exactly where 'recompute RectTransform layout from CurrentRes/GlobalScale and parent->child rects' belongs. Add e.g. UIManager/UI-layout-system->LayoutTick(dt) as a new line at Engine.cpp:178. This also lets in-game drag (which only mutates Element.position) feed the same pass each frame. Because TickFrameBody is the shared base used by both UEngine::Tick and UGameEngine::Tick, one hook covers game + base paths; the editor engine would need the analogous spot in its own tick body.

evidence.note: TickFrameBody: insert UI LayoutTick on a new line between WorldTick(DeltaTime) (177) and Render(DeltaTime) (178)
UGameEngine::Tick calls TickFrameBody(dt), so the same hook serves the standalone game path

findings.conclusion: SECONDARY candidate: hook the layout pass at the tail of UWorld::Tick (after TickPlayerCamera, World.cpp:672) so it runs per-world inside the existing world tick. Weaker than the TickFrameBody hook because (a) the new UI model is Canvas/Group/Element, a screen-space singleton, not naturally per-world; (b) it runs once per FWorldContext (multiple worlds in editor/PIE) which is wrong for a single screen-space canvas; and (c) UWorld::Tick early-returns when bPaused (World.cpp:624-628), which would freeze UI layout while paused — undesirable for menus. Prefer the engine-level TickFrameBody hook.

evidence.note: End of UWorld::Tick after TickPlayerCamera() — per-world, runs once per FWorldContext
UWorld::Tick early-returns on bPaused, which would freeze any UI layout placed after this point

findings.conclusion: TERTIARY candidate (NOT recommended as the layout home): fold layout into the render pass, mirroring how RmlUi does its update at UIManager.cpp:831 / UIPass.cpp:27. A new FRenderPass (e.g. inserted at ERenderPass::UI order in RenderTypes.h) could call the simple UI system's layout+draw. This works and matches the existing pattern, but conflates layout (CPU, game-state-dependent) with GPU submission, runs after the frame's transforms are already consumed by 3D passes, and makes the layout result unavailable to game logic in the same frame. Recommend: compute layout in TickFrameBody (primary), and add only a thin DRAW-only render pass alongside/after FUIPass for the new model. Decision for the design doc: the NEW simple model should be a SEPARATE lightweight system with its own game-thread LayoutTick + its own draw pass, coexisting with (not reusing) the RmlUi update-in-render path.

evidence.note: REGISTER_RENDER_PASS(FUIPass); Execute calls UUIManager::Render — the existing render-pass-driven UI pattern to optionally mirror for DRAW only
ERenderPass::UI slot; a new simple-UI draw pass would register near here in enum order
Existing conflation of Update()+Render() inside a render pass — the anti-pattern to avoid for the new model's layout

openQuestions: Editor path: UEditorEngine likely has its own tick body (EditorEngine.cpp references UUIManager::Get().ClearViewport() at line 500) — confirm whether it routes through UEngine::TickFrameBody or a separate editor tick, so the layout hook covers in-editor UI authoring too.

FGameRenderPipeline::Execute renders the world into a viewport SRV and then BlitToBackBuffer — confirm whether UIPass draws into the viewport RTV (pre-blit, so UI gets scaled/letterboxed with the world) or should instead draw post-blit at native back-buffer resolution; this affects whether GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080 must account for the viewport-vs-backbuffer resolution.

Verify whether the new simple UI's input (mouse drag) should read from the game-thread input stage (TickFrameStart / GameViewportClient->ProcessInput) rather than the render-time UUIManager::ProcessInput(Frame) path the RmlUi UI currently uses, to keep drag-to-move on the game thread.

verdicts.claim: [A3] RmlUi UIManager is not ticked relative to game tick; no per-frame Tick/Update in Runtime (only Initialize at Engine.cpp:102, Shutdown at Engine.cpp:120). Only per-frame UI driver is FUIPass::Execute -> UUIManager::Render inside the render pass (UIPass.cpp:25-28), where SetDimensions/ProcessInput(Frame)/RmlContext->Update()/RmlContext->Render() all run together (UIManager.cpp:811-835). Input pulled directly inside Render via ProcessInput(Frame), not from game-thread input stage.
verdicts.note: All evidence verified exactly. UIPass.cpp:25-28: FUIPass::Execute calls UUIManager::Get().Render(Ctx) (line 27). UIManager.cpp:811-835: UUIManager::Render does SetDimensions (819-822), ProcessInput(Ctx.Frame) at 824, RmlContext->Update() at 831, RmlContext->Render() at 833 -- update+render conflated in render pass. Engine.cpp:102 Initialize, 120 Shutdown. Grep for UUIManager::Get() in Runtime returns ONLY those two lines (102, 120) -- no per-frame Tick. Grep for UUIManager::Get().(Render|Update|Tick|ProcessInput) engine-wide returns ONLY UIPass.cpp:27 -- confirming Render is the sole per-frame UI driver and runs late in the render pass. ProcessInput(Frame) confirmed at UIManager.cpp:837 reading directly from InputSystem inside Render path, not from a game-thread input stage.

verdicts.claim: [C1] Primary insertion point for a new UI Layout Pass is inside UEngine::TickFrameBody between WorldTick(DeltaTime) (Engine.cpp:177) and Render(DeltaTime) (Engine.cpp:178), i.e. a new line at Engine.cpp:178. Runs on game thread after gameplay ticks settle and before render submission. TickFrameBody is shared base for both UEngine::Tick and UGameEngine::Tick (GameEngine.cpp:88 calls TickFrameBody(dt)), so one hook covers game+base paths; editor engine needs analogous spot in its own tick body.
verdicts.note: Verified. Engine.cpp:174-179 TickFrameBody: FAudioManager::Get().Tick() (176), WorldTick(DeltaTime) (177), Render(DeltaTime) (178) -- inserting a LayoutTick on a new line between 177 and 178 is exactly between WorldTick and Render, on the game thread before render submission, as claimed. GameEngine.cpp:88: UGameEngine::Tick calls TickFrameBody(DeltaTime), confirming the standalone game path shares the same hook. UEngine::Tick (Engine.cpp:201) also calls TickFrameBody, confirming shared base. The 'editor engine needs analogous spot' caveat is supported: UObjViewerEngine::Tick (ObjViewerEngine.cpp:63-73) is a separate override that does its own pre-body work but also routes through TickFrameBody(DeltaTime) at line 72 -- so it would actually share the same hook, but the claim's caution about separate engine tick bodies is reasonable and not contradicted.

dimensions.dimension: A4 — math types available for the UI (Vec2 / Rect)
dimensions.summary: The engine has a usable 2D float vector (FVector2 in Engine/Math/Vector.h, line 222) and a single pos/size rectangle struct (FRect in Editor/Slate/SWindow.h, line 11), but there is NO integer 2D point type (no FIntPoint) and NO component-wise vector multiply. FVector2 supports X/Y members, +/- (vector and scalar), and *,/ by SCALAR only — the intended layout math AnchorPx = ParentSize*anchor and size*pivot are component-wise products and CANNOT be expressed with FVector2's existing operator*; they must be written manually as FVector2{A.X*B.X, A.Y*B.Y} (the codebase already does this ad hoc, e.g. ParticleVertexFactory.cpp:440, and has a local FVector(3D) ComponentMultiply helper in FbxAnimationImporter.cpp:891 but nothing for FVector2). The only FRect is in the Editor/Slate layer and is shape-compatible (X,Y,Width,Height = origin+size) with Element rects, but living under Editor/Slate makes it an awkward dependency for a runtime/Engine UI; a small Engine-side rect or just storing pos+size as two FVector2 is cleaner. Window/viewport resolution is carried as raw floats (e.g. UIManager Frame.ViewportWidth/Height are float), so GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080 has no native int-point source — float dimensions are the norm.

findings.conclusion: A 2D float vector FVector2 exists at Engine/Math/Vector.h:222. Members: X,Y (also aliased U,V and Data[2] via a union). Constructors: default (0,0) and (InX,InY). Methods: Length, Normalize, Normalized, Dot. This is sufficient as the Vec2 type for pivot/anchor/position/size in the new UI model.

evidence.note: struct FVector2 with union{X,Y / U,V / Data[2]}, ctors, Length/Normalize/Dot and operator declarations

findings.conclusion: FVector2 has +,- with both another FVector2 and a scalar, and *,/ with a SCALAR ONLY. There is NO operator* taking an FVector2 (no component-wise/Hadamard product) and no member ComponentMul. Therefore AnchorPx=ParentSize*anchor and size*pivot (both component-wise) CANNOT use FVector2::operator* and must be done manually: FVector2{ParentSize.X*anchor.X, ParentSize.Y*anchor.Y}. The codebase already writes such per-component products inline (e.g. FVector2{P.Size.X*SizeScale.X, P.Size.Y*SizeScale.Y}) and has a free ComponentMultiply only for the 3D FVector, not FVector2.

evidence.note: operator+ / operator- have FVector2 and float overloads; operator* and operator/ take only float Scalar — no FVector2 overload
operator*(float) and operator/(float) implementations multiply/divide both X and Y by the scalar; confirms scalar-only
existing manual component-wise FVector2 multiply: FVector2{ P.Size.X * SizeScale.X, P.Size.Y * SizeScale.Y } — precedent for how the UI must do it
static FVector ComponentMultiply(A,B) returns FVector(A.X*B.X,...) — a 3D helper exists but nothing equivalent for FVector2

findings.conclusion: There is NO integer 2D point type (no FIntPoint, no FIntVector2) in the engine source. Grep for FIntPoint returns no engine matches (only RmlUi thirdparty has its own MyIntVector2). Window/viewport/screen sizes are stored and passed as raw floats: UIManager's per-frame Frame.ViewportWidth/ViewportHeight are float, ObjViewerViewportClient.ViewportWidth is float (800.0f). So for the GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080 computation there is no native int point to read from — the resolution is already float-typed, which is fine for the divide.

evidence.note: float ViewportWidth = 1.0f; float ViewportHeight = 1.0f; — viewport resolution stored as float, cast to int only when handing to Rml (lines 820-821)
float ViewportWidth = 800.0f; — viewport dimensions are floats engine-wide

findings.conclusion: There is exactly ONE real FRect definition: Editor/Slate/SWindow.h:11, shape {float X, float Y, float Width, float Height} — i.e. origin+size (pos/size), NOT min/max. The forward-decls in OverlayStatSystem.h:7 and Slate/SWindow.h:11 and the use in GameViewportClient.h all resolve to this same struct (those .h/.cpp include Slate/SWindow.h). SWindow.h also defines a sibling FPoint{float X,Y}. The pos/size shape is directly reusable for an Element rect (origin = FinalPos, size = size), and FinalPos = ParentOrigin + AnchorPx + position - size*pivot maps cleanly onto X/Y/Width/Height. CAVEAT: FRect lives in the Editor/Slate layer (Slate window layout), so a runtime Engine-side UI reusing it would create an Engine->Editor/Slate dependency; cleaner to define a tiny Engine-side rect or just keep pos+size as two FVector2 in the Element.

evidence.note: the only real FRect: struct FRect { float X,Y,Width,Height = 0 }; pos/size form, not min/max
sibling struct FPoint { float X,Y }; used as a screen-space 2D point in Slate/viewport input
forward-decl 'struct FRect;' — resolved via Slate/SWindow.h include in OverlayStatSystem.cpp:16 to the same definition
#include "Slate/SWindow.h" then SetCursorClipRect(const FRect&) — Engine code already pulls Editor/Slate's FRect, showing the cross-layer dependency that reuse would entail

openQuestions: The single FRect lives under Editor/Slate; the design must decide whether the runtime UI may depend on that layer or should introduce a small Engine-level rect (or store pos+size as two FVector2). Whether RmlUi's own Vector2/Rectangle types (ThirdParty/RmlUi) should be touched at all depends on the separate-vs-reuse decision for the RmlUi path (out of scope for A4).

No constexpr/aggregate-init niceties on FVector2 (it has user-defined ctors), so brace-init like FVector2{a,b} works but it is not a literal type — minor, only matters if compile-time constants are wanted for the 1920x1080 reference resolution.

verdicts.claim: [A4-component-mul-missing] FVector2 has +,- with FVector2 and scalar, *,/ scalar-only; no operator* taking FVector2 and no ComponentMul; component-wise products must be inline; precedent exists; ComponentMultiply only for 3D FVector.
verdicts.note: Vector.h:248-253 verified: operator+(const FVector2&), operator-(const FVector2&), operator+(float), operator-(float), operator*(float Scalar), operator/(float Scalar) — only scalar overloads for * and /. Compound ops (256-259) likewise scalar-only for *=,/=. Vector.cpp:386-398 confirms operator*(float)/operator/(float) scale both X and Y by the scalar. ParticleVertexFactory.cpp:440 is the exact inline precedent: FVector2{ P.Size.X*SizeScale.X, P.Size.Y*SizeScale.Y }. FbxAnimationImporter.cpp:891-893 is a static FVector (3D) ComponentMultiply. Grep across Source confirms ComponentMultiply appears ONLY in FbxAnimationImporter.cpp (def line 891, use line 1000) and no FVector2 variant; grep for 'FVector2 operator*(const FVector2' and 'operator\* ... FVector2' in Math returns no matches. Negative assertion fully holds.

verdicts.claim: [A4-FRect-real-definition] Exactly one real FRect at Slate/SWindow.h:11, shape {float X,Y,Width,Height} = pos/size not min/max; forward-decls/uses resolve to it; sibling FPoint{X,Y}; FRect in Editor/Slate layer so Engine reuse creates Engine->Editor dependency (GameViewportClient already pulls it).
verdicts.note: SWindow.h:11-17 verified as the sole definition: struct FRect { float X=0; float Y=0; float Width=0; float Height=0; } — pos/size origin+size form, not min/max. SWindow.h:5-9 sibling struct FPoint { float X,Y }. OverlayStatSystem.h:7 is forward-decl 'struct FRect;'; OverlayStatSystem.cpp:16 includes "Slate/SWindow.h", resolving it. GameViewportClient.h:5 #include "Slate/SWindow.h" and line 44 SetCursorClipRect(const FRect& InViewportScreenRect) — Engine code pulling Editor/Slate FRect, confirming the cross-layer dependency. Grep shows 13 files reference FRect but only one struct definition (SWindow.h:11) plus one forward-decl (OverlayStatSystem.h:7); SSplitter.h merely uses FRect, no second definition. Cited line numbers all accurate.

dimensions.dimension: A5 (mouse input path / coordinate space) + E3 (game-vs-editor input separation, mode/capture toggle)
dimensions.summary: Mouse input in this engine is POLLING-based, not message-dispatch-based. The Win32 message pump (FWindowsApplication::WndProc) only forwards WM_MOUSEWHEEL (scroll) and WM_INPUT (raw delta for FPS mouselook) into InputSystem; it never handles WM_MOUSEMOVE/WM_LBUTTONDOWN etc. Instead, InputSystem::Tick() polls GetCursorPos + GetAsyncKeyState every frame and exposes button down/held/released, mouse position, frame delta, and a built-in drag-tracker (start/dragging/end + drag vector with a 5px threshold). The single canonical place to read mouse position is InputSystem::Get().GetMouseClientPos() which returns CLIENT pixels, origin top-left (it calls Win32 ScreenToClient on the owner HWND) — this is the hit-test foundation. Crucially for the project: a full UI-vs-game input arbitration layer ALREADY EXISTS for the RmlUi UI. UUIManager::GetViewportInputCaptureState() aggregates per-widget flags (bWantsMouse/bWantsTextInput/bBlocksGameInput/bBlocksGameMouseLook/bBlocksGameKeyboard) into FUIInputCaptureState, and UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput consumes it to gate/strip the per-frame game snapshot before it reaches the PlayerController, plus there is an EGameInputMode {GameOnly,GameAndUI,UIOnly} enum and a PIE Possessed/Ejected toggle (F8). A UI-editor-mode toggle should reuse this exact pattern (set BlocksGameInput / a new EGameInputMode or a manager flag) rather than inventing a new capture path.

findings.conclusion: Mouse input does NOT arrive via per-message WndProc handling of WM_MOUSEMOVE/WM_LBUTTONDOWN. The window proc (FWindowsApplication::WndProc) only handles WM_MOUSEWHEEL -> InputSystem::AddScrollDelta and WM_INPUT (raw mouse) -> InputSystem::AddRawMouseDelta. All other mouse messages fall through to ImGui's handler / DefWindowProc. Confirmed there is zero WM_LBUTTONDOWN/WM_MOUSEMOVE handling anywhere in engine source (only a doc comment in OverlayStatSystem.h).

evidence.note: WndProc switch: only WM_DESTROY, WM_MOUSEWHEEL (line 40-42), WM_INPUT (line 43-65), WM_SIZE/sizing. No mouse-move/button cases.
ImGui_ImplWin32_WndProcHandler is called first; bImGuiHandled returned for wheel/raw so ImGui gets a chance, but engine button/pos state is independent of it.
PumpMessages(): standard PeekMessage/TranslateMessage/DispatchMessage loop; sets bIsExitRequested on WM_QUIT.

findings.conclusion: Mouse position and button state are POLLED each frame, not event-driven. InputSystem::Tick() loops VK 0..255 calling GetAsyncKeyState (this is where LBUTTON/RBUTTON/MBUTTON down state comes from) and calls GetCursorPos for screen-space MousePos, computing FrameMouseDelta. So 'button down/pressed/released' = derived from GetAsyncKeyState edge detection (CurrentStates vs PrevStates), NOT from WM_*BUTTON* messages.

evidence.note: for i in 0..255: PrevStates[i]=CurrentStates[i]; CurrentStates[i]=(GetAsyncKeyState(i)&0x8000)!=0; — polls all keys incl. VK_LBUTTON.
PrevMousePos=MousePos; GetCursorPos(&MousePos); FrameMouseDeltaX/Y computed from screen-px delta.
InputSystem::Get().Tick() called once per frame in UEngine::TickFrameStart — the single poll point.

findings.conclusion: Concrete read APIs available to consumers (all on InputSystem singleton via InputSystem::Get()): position GetMousePos() (screen px) and GetMouseClientPos() (client px, top-left origin); per-frame delta MouseDeltaX()/MouseDeltaY()/MouseMoved(); buttons GetKey(VK_LBUTTON)/GetKeyDown/GetKeyUp (down/held-vs-pressed-edge/released-edge); drag GetLeftDragStart()/GetLeftDragging()/GetLeftDragEnd()/GetLeftDragVector()/GetLeftDragDistance() (+Right variants) with a built-in DRAG_THRESHOLD=5px. Same data is also packaged into FInputSystemSnapshot (MousePos, MouseDeltaX/Y, bLeftMousePressed/Down/Released, bLeftDragStarted/Dragging/Ended, LeftDragVector) via MakeSnapshot(). For the in-game UI editor drag feature, GetLeftDragging()+GetLeftDragVector() (or snapshot bLeftDragging+LeftDragVector) is the ready-made drag-to-move primitive.

evidence.note: GetMousePos() (screen), GetMouseClientPos() (client), MouseDeltaX/Y(), MouseMoved().
GetKeyDown/GetKey/GetKeyUp edge+held accessors; pass VK_LBUTTON/VK_RBUTTON for buttons.
Left/Right drag API: DragStart/Dragging/End + GetLeftDragVector/Distance.
FilterDragThreshold (DRAG_THRESHOLD 5px, DistSq>=25) + GetLeftDragVector = MousePos - LeftDragStartPos.
FInputSystemSnapshot: full per-frame mouse/button/drag snapshot consumed by gameplay.

findings.conclusion: Mouse coordinate space for hit-testing: GetMousePos() returns SCREEN/desktop pixels (raw GetCursorPos). GetMouseClientPos() returns CLIENT pixels with origin at window top-left (it applies ScreenToClient on OwnerHWnd). The existing RmlUi UI already hit-tests using exactly GetMouseClientPos() (UIManager.cpp:856). In STANDALONE game the viewport fills the client area, so client-px == viewport-px directly (GameRenderPipeline.cpp:203-210 feeds GetMouseClientPos straight into CursorViewportX/Y, origin top-left, after a >=0 / <ViewportWidth/Height bounds check). In the EDITOR the game view is a sub-rect, so a transform (subtract ViewportScreenRect origin) is required — done via VC->GetCursorViewportPosition (EditorRenderPipeline.cpp:361) and ImGui MousePos minus ViewportScreenRect in EditorViewportClient.cpp:590-592. For the new 1920x1080 reference-resolution UI, hit-testing must use client/viewport px then divide by GlobalScale (CurrentRes.Y/1080) to map into reference space.

evidence.note: GetMouseClientPos(): copies MousePos then ScreenToClient(OwnerHWnd) -> client px, top-left origin. OwnerHWnd set via SetOwnerWindow.
Existing RmlUi path: const POINT MousePos = Input.GetMouseClientPos(); MouseX/Y = MousePos.x/y — proof client-px is the established UI hit-test space.
Standalone: CursorViewportX/Y = GetMouseClientPos() directly when within [0,ViewportWidth/Height) — client px == viewport px, top-left origin.
Editor sub-rect: LocalMouseX = ImGui MousePos.x - ViewportScreenRect.X — viewport-local coords require subtracting the panel origin.

findings.conclusion: Game-vs-UI input separation ALREADY EXISTS and is the pattern the new UI-editor toggle must hook into. UUIManager::GetViewportInputCaptureState() aggregates (logical-OR) per-widget flags into FUIInputCaptureState {bWantsMouse, bWantsKeyboard, bWantsTextInput, bBlocksGameInput, bBlocksGameKeyboard, bBlocksGameMouseLook}. UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput pulls this every frame and: (a) early-returns (no game snapshot) if InputMode==UIOnly or bBlocksGameInput; (b) strips mouse keys from the snapshot if bWantsMouse||bBlocksGameMouseLook; (c) strips keyboard if bWantsTextInput||bBlocksGameKeyboard; (d) only the filtered GameSnapshot reaches PlayerController->ProcessPlayerInput. So 'stealing mouse from gameplay' is already implemented as: a UI widget sets WantsMouse/BlocksGameInput, and the viewport client withholds those inputs from the game.

evidence.note: ProcessInput: gets FUIInputCaptureState (109), ApplyGameCapturePolicy (110), UIOnly/bBlocksGameInput early-return (112-115), ClearMouseInput if bWantsMouse||bBlocksGameMouseLook (118/121-124), ClearKeyboardInput if bWantsTextInput||bBlocksGameKeyboard (119/125-128), then SetGameInputSnapshot (130).
GetViewportInputCaptureState ORs each widget's WantsMouse/WantsTextInput/BlocksGameInput/BlocksGameKeyboard/BlocksGameMouseLook.
Per-widget setters SetWantsMouse/SetBlocksGameInput/SetBlocksGameMouseLook etc. — the API a UI editor would call to steal input.
enum class EGameInputMode { GameOnly, GameAndUI, UIOnly } — existing input-mode concept; SetInputMode at .h:51.
ProcessActiveWorldPlayerInput -> PlayerController->ProcessPlayerInput consumes only the (already-filtered) GameInputSnapshot.

findings.conclusion: Editor/tool input vs game is separated via ImGui's WantCaptureMouse/WantCaptureKeyboard/WantTextInput, mirrored into InputSystem's FGuiInputState. ImGui_ImplWin32_WndProcHandler runs first in WndProc (consuming events for ImGui windows). Each frame the editor copies IO.WantCaptureMouse/Keyboard/TextInput into InputSystem::GetGuiInputState() and into FSlateApplication. Note: this GuiState mirror is NOT what gates the game snapshot (that's the UUIManager FUIInputCaptureState path above); GuiState/WantTextInput is used ad hoc by editor widgets to suppress their own shortcuts. So there are effectively TWO arbitration layers: ImGui (editor tools) and UUIManager (in-game RmlUi UI). The new simple UI model conceptually belongs with the latter.

evidence.note: Per-frame mirror: InputSystem GuiState.bUsingMouse=IO.WantCaptureMouse; .bUsingKeyboard=IO.WantCaptureKeyboard||overlay; .bUsingTextInput=IO.WantTextInput; FSlateApplication::SetTextInputActive.
FGuiInputState GetGuiInputState()/SetGuiMouseCapture/IsGuiUsingMouse — the ImGui mirror store.
ImGui handler invoked first each message so ImGui windows capture before engine sees the message.

findings.conclusion: A precedent input-mode ON/OFF toggle already exists: PIE Possessed vs Ejected mode (EPIEControlMode), toggled by F8. UEditorEngine::TogglePIEControlMode -> EnterPIEPossessedMode/EnterPIEEjectedMode flips SetUseRawMouse, syncs viewport possess state, and resets transient input. This is the exact shape a 'UI-editor-mode' toggle should take: a single enum/bool, a toggle key, and on enter/exit it (a) flips whether the game viewport is possessed, (b) sets BlocksGameInput / EGameInputMode::UIOnly so gameplay stops receiving mouse, (c) releases raw-mouse capture and shows the cursor (UGameViewportClient::SetCursorCaptured/ReleaseGameCapture). The toggle logic would live next to UEditorEngine PIE-mode code or in UUIManager, and steal mouse input by routing it to the UI editor instead of the filtered GameInputSnapshot.

evidence.note: TogglePIEControlMode + EnterPIEPossessedMode/EnterPIEEjectedMode: flip PIEControlMode, SyncGameViewportPIEControlState, SetUseRawMouse(true/false), ResetTransientState, ClearInputOwner.
F8 (VK_F8) in ProcessPIEInput calls TogglePIEControlMode — example of a mode hotkey.
ReleaseGameCapture/ApplyGameCapturePolicy: SetUseRawMouse(false)+SetCursorCaptured(false) shows cursor; this is how a UI-editor mode would free the mouse for dragging.
EPIEControlMode {Possessed,Ejected} enum + IsPIEPossessedMode/Ejected + TogglePIEControlMode declaration — template for a UI-edit-mode flag.

openQuestions: The new simple UI model: does it render through the existing RmlUi UUIManager path (and thus get GetViewportInputCaptureState input arbitration for free), or is it a separate lightweight overlay? If separate, it must replicate the FUIInputCaptureState->UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput gating so it can steal the mouse from gameplay; GuiState (ImGui mirror) alone does NOT gate the game snapshot.

GetMouseClientPos() applies ScreenToClient on InputSystem's OwnerHWnd — confirm OwnerHWnd is set to the main game window in standalone (SetOwnerWindow caller not inspected here); if unset, GetMouseClientPos returns screen px unchanged.

Drag tracking (GetLeftDragVector) is in SCREEN px (LeftDragStartPos/MousePos are GetCursorPos values, not client-converted). For UI-editor drag-to-move in reference space, the delta must be divided by GlobalScale; raw screen-px and client-px deltas are numerically equal (translation cancels) so this is fine, but the absolute drag-start hit-test must use GetMouseClientPos.

verdicts.claim: [A5-pump] Mouse input does NOT arrive via per-message WndProc handling of WM_MOUSEMOVE/WM_LBUTTONDOWN. WndProc only handles WM_MOUSEWHEEL->AddScrollDelta and WM_INPUT->AddRawMouseDelta; all other mouse messages fall through to ImGui/DefWindowProc. Zero WM_LBUTTONDOWN/WM_MOUSEMOVE handling anywhere (only a doc comment in OverlayStatSystem.h). PumpMessages is a standard PeekMessage/Translate/Dispatch loop setting bIsExitRequested on WM_QUIT.
verdicts.note: All verified. WindowsApplication.cpp:31-100 WndProc switch handles only WM_DESTROY(37-39), WM_MOUSEWHEEL(40-42)->InputSystem::AddScrollDelta, WM_INPUT(43-65) raw mouse->AddRawMouseDelta, WM_SIZE/ENTERSIZEMOVE/EXITSIZEMOVE/SIZING. No WM_MOUSEMOVE/WM_LBUTTONDOWN cases; default->ImGui/DefWindowProc. Line 33: ImGui_ImplWin32_WndProcHandler called first, bImGuiHandled returned for wheel/raw. PumpMessages at 144-158 matches exactly. Grep across entire Source tree for WM_LBUTTONDOWN/WM_MOUSEMOVE/etc found ONLY OverlayStatSystem.h:80 (a comment), confirming zero handling. Note: cited line 31-99 should be 31-100 for the closing brace, but content is accurate.

verdicts.claim: [A5-coord-space] GetMousePos returns screen px (raw GetCursorPos); GetMouseClientPos returns client px via ScreenToClient(OwnerHWnd). Existing RmlUi UI hit-tests using exactly GetMouseClientPos (UIManager.cpp:856). Standalone: client-px==viewport-px, GetMouseClientPos fed into CursorViewportX/Y after bounds check (GameRenderPipeline.cpp:203-210). Editor: sub-rect transform via ImGui MousePos minus ViewportScreenRect (EditorViewportClient.cpp:590-592).
verdicts.note: Coordinate-space mechanics fully confirmed. InputSystem.h:85 GetMousePos returns MousePos; 86-94 GetMouseClientPos applies ScreenToClient(OwnerHWnd). GameRenderPipeline.cpp:203-210 confirmed: GetMouseClientPos with >=0 and <ViewportWidth/Height bounds check into CursorViewportX/Y (else UINT32_MAX). EditorViewportClient.cpp:590-592 confirmed: LocalMouseX = ImGui MousePos.x - ViewportScreenRect.X. PARTIAL because the claim overstates UIManager: it says RmlUi 'hit-tests using exactly GetMouseClientPos() (UIManager.cpp:856).' In fact UIManager.cpp:849-859 PREFERS Frame.CursorViewportX/Y when valid (!=UINT32_MAX) and only FALLS BACK to GetMouseClientPos() at 856. Since CursorViewportX/Y is itself derived from GetMouseClientPos (GameRenderPipeline 208-209), the established hit-test space is still client/viewport px, so the conclusion holds, but 'exactly GetMouseClientPos' is the fallback branch, not the primary path. The GetMousePos==raw GetCursorPos detail and the GlobalScale (CurrentRes.Y/1080) prescription were not independently checked against source (the GlobalScale part is a forward-looking recommendation, not a code claim).

verdicts.claim: [E3-existing-arbitration] Game-vs-UI input separation already exists. GetViewportInputCaptureState ORs per-widget flags into FUIInputCaptureState{bWantsMouse,bWantsKeyboard,bWantsTextInput,bBlocksGameInput,bBlocksGameKeyboard,bBlocksGameMouseLook}. ProcessInput pulls it every frame: early-returns if UIOnly||bBlocksGameInput; strips mouse if bWantsMouse||bBlocksGameMouseLook; strips keyboard if bWantsTextInput||bBlocksGameKeyboard; only filtered GameSnapshot reaches PlayerController->ProcessPlayerInput. Per-widget setters exist; enum EGameInputMode{GameOnly,GameAndUI,UIOnly}.
verdicts.note: All verified. GameViewportClient.cpp:92-131 ProcessInput: gets UIState(109), ApplyGameCapturePolicy(110), UIOnly||bBlocksGameInput early-return(112-115), bBlockGameMouse=bWantsMouse||bBlocksGameMouseLook->ClearMouseInput(118/121-124), bBlockGameKeyboard=bWantsTextInput||bBlocksGameKeyboard->ClearKeyboardInput(119/125-128), SetGameInputSnapshot(GameSnapshot)(130). UIManager.cpp:642-660 ORs all six flags. UserWidget.h:94-141 has SetWantsMouse/SetWantsKeyboard/SetWantsTextInput/SetBlocksGameInput/SetBlocksGameKeyboard/SetBlocksGameMouseLook. GameViewportClient.h:19-24 enum EGameInputMode{GameOnly,GameAndUI,UIOnly}; SetInputMode at .h:51. Engine.cpp:181-196 ProcessActiveWorldPlayerInput->PlayerController->ProcessPlayerInput; and callers GameEngine.cpp:77 and EditorEngine.cpp:240 pass GameViewportClient->GetGameInputSnapshot() (the filtered snapshot set at GameViewportClient.cpp:130/275), confirming only the filtered snapshot reaches the PlayerController. Minor: claim lists bWantsKeyboard in the struct (true, present at UIManager.cpp:653) though it is not consumed by ProcessInput's filtering logic.

dimensions.dimension: B (render path): screen-space 2D draw path, coordinate conventions, scale application (B1, B2, B4, D3)
dimensions.summary: This engine already has a complete, production screen-space 2D quad draw path: the RmlUi D3D11 RenderInterface (KraftonEngine/Source/Engine/UI/UIManager.cpp) that compiles textured/colored vertex+index quads and draws them via DrawIndexed, plus a second simpler analog (FFontGeometry screen text). Both submit colored/textured triangle lists with NoDepth+AlphaBlend+SolidNoCull and do pixel->NDC entirely in the vertex shader using a top-left-origin, Y-down pixel convention (the engine's universal 2D convention, also used by the fullscreen-triangle helper). UI is submitted as a dedicated render pass (FUIPass, ERenderPass::UI) that runs late in the enum-ordered pass pipeline: after all 3D/postprocess/bloom/FXAA and after OverlayFont, and just before GammaCorrection. The pass executes against the viewport RTV with viewport TopLeftX/Y=0 and Width/Height = full viewport size. There is NO existing GlobalScale / reference-resolution / UI-scale concept anywhere. RECOMMENDATION (D3): apply GlobalScale at the layout stage (multiply final pixel rects) rather than baking it into the ortho/vertex transform, because the existing 2D path carries no projection matrix — pixel->NDC is a hardcoded divide-by-ViewportSize in the shader with only an optional per-call Translation and an identity-by-default Transform — and the simpler FFontGeometry path already scales geometry in pixel space (Scale multiplies CharW/CharH before pixel->clip). Either system the new UI model targets can drive this draw path directly by emitting scaled pixel rects.

findings.conclusion: Yes — the closest existing analog to a UI quad batch is the RmlUi D3D11 RenderInterface. FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11::CompileGeometry builds an interleaved POSITION(float2)+COLOR(float4)+TEXCOORD(float2) vertex buffer + R32 index buffer, and RenderGeometry binds the 'Shaders/UI/RmlUi.hlsl' shader, sets NoDepth/AlphaBlend/SolidNoCull, binds the viewport RTV/DSV, and issues DrawIndexed for each batch. This is a fully generic textured/colored 2D quad submit and is exactly where a new UI quad batch would be drawn. A second, simpler analog is FFontGeometry::AddScreenText, which builds screen-space textured quads (4 verts/6 idx per char) directly in clip space. A new lightweight UI model can either reuse the RmlUi RenderInterface's quad-submit machinery or, more cleanly, mirror the FFontGeometry pattern (build pixel-rect quads -> upload VB/IB -> draw) without pulling in RmlUi.

evidence.note: CompileGeometry: converts Rml vertices to FRmlVertexD3D11{X,Y,R,G,B,A,U,V}, creates VB+IB — the textured-quad batch builder
RenderGeometry: binds UI shader, NoDepth/AlphaBlend/SolidNoCull, OMSetRenderTargets(Cache.RTV/DSV), DrawIndexed — the actual 2D quad submit
AddScreenText: simpler screen-space quad builder, 4 verts/6 idx per glyph, an alternative analog without RmlUi

findings.conclusion: Coordinate convention is top-left origin, Y-DOWN in pixels — matching the target model exactly. pixel->NDC conversion happens entirely in the vertex shader, not via a projection matrix. In Shaders/UI/RmlUi.hlsl VS: NdcPosition.x = (PixelPos.x/ViewportSize.x)*2-1; NdcPosition.y = 1 - (PixelPos.y/ViewportSize.y)*2 — i.e. pixel (0,0)=top-left maps to NDC (-1,+1)=top-left. The identical formula appears in FFontGeometry::AddScreenText (PixelToClipX/PixelToClipY lambdas), and the engine's FullscreenTriangleVS (Functions.hlsli) also uses Y-flip (uv*float2(2,-2)+float2(-1,1)), so uv.y=0 -> NDC +1 = top. The D3D11 swapchain/viewport is set TopLeftX/Y=0, Width/Height=full size, MinDepth0/MaxDepth1 (FD3DDevice::ViewportInfo and FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11::BeginFrame), which is the standard D3D top-left raster origin. No ortho matrix is involved in the 2D path: the RmlUi CB carries ViewportSize, a per-draw Translation, and a Transform that defaults to identity.

evidence.note: VS pixel->NDC: x=(px/W)*2-1, y=1-(py/H)*2 — top-left origin, Y-down pixels; no projection matrix
PixelToClipX/Y lambdas: identical Y-flip pixel->clip formula, confirming engine-wide top-left/Y-down 2D convention
FullscreenTriangleVS: position = uv*float2(2,-2)+float2(-1,1) — uv.y=0 maps to NDC top, same Y-down screen convention
ViewportInfo = {0,0,W,H,0,1} top-left origin D3D11 viewport
RmlUi BeginFrame sets D3D11_VIEWPORT TopLeftX/Y=0, full Width/Height

findings.conclusion: UI is submitted as a dedicated render pass FUIPass (PassType = ERenderPass::UI), registered via REGISTER_RENDER_PASS and run by FRenderPassPipeline::Execute. Passes are sorted by ERenderPass enum value (RenderPassRegistry::CreateAll, std::sort), and in the enum UI sits near the very end: ...PostProcess, Bloom, FXAA, EditorIcon, GizmoOuter, GizmoInner, OverlayFont, UI, GammaCorrection. So UI draws AFTER all 3D geometry, after postprocess/bloom/FXAA, after the OverlayFont screen-space text, and immediately BEFORE final GammaCorrection — i.e. composited late onto the viewport RTV. The pass chain runs inside FRenderer::Render (Pipeline.Execute(PassCtx)), which is called before Renderer.EndFrame()->Device.Present(). FUIPass::BeginPass gates on Frame.ViewportRTV && UUIManager::Get().HasViewportWidgets(); Execute calls UUIManager::Get().Render(Ctx), which runs RmlContext->Update() then RenderInterface->BeginFrame/Render/EndFrame. A new UI model should hook in at this same site — either as a new ERenderPass entry adjacent to UI, or by extending FUIPass/UUIManager::Render. Note GammaCorrection runs AFTER UI, so UI colors are written in linear space and will be gamma-corrected with the scene (relevant for color matching, not for layout).

evidence.note: FUIPass::BeginPass gates on ViewportRTV+HasViewportWidgets; Execute -> UUIManager::Get().Render(Ctx)
ERenderPass enum order: PostProcess, Bloom, FXAA, EditorIcon, GizmoOuter, GizmoInner, OverlayFont, UI, GammaCorrection, MAX — UI is second-to-last
CreateAll sorts passes by ERenderPass value: 'enum 순서 = 실행 순서' (enum order == execution order)
Execute iterates passes in sorted order calling BeginPass/Execute/EndPass
Pipeline.Execute(PassCtx) in Render(); EndFrame()->Device.Present() — UI is drawn before Present
UUIManager::Render: SetDimensions(viewport), Update, RenderInterface BeginFrame/Render/EndFrame

findings.conclusion: Apply GlobalScale at the LAYOUT stage (multiply the final pixel rects / sizes / positions), NOT in an ortho projection or vertex transform. Rationale grounded in the existing path: (1) There is NO ortho matrix to scale — the 2D path converts pixels to NDC with a hardcoded divide-by-ViewportSize in the shader (RmlUi.hlsl VS, FontGeometry PixelToClip), so 'scaling the projection' would mean editing shader math, whereas pixel-space scaling needs no render changes. (2) The existing simpler 2D path already scales in pixel space: FFontGeometry::AddScreenText multiplies CharW/CharH = 23.0*Scale BEFORE the pixel->clip conversion — precedent for 'scale the geometry pixels, then convert.' (3) The RmlUi CB exposes a per-draw Transform (default identity) and Translation, but RmlUi computes its own pixel layout from context dimensions, so a global scale belongs in the layout computation (AnchorPx/FinalPos = ParentSize*anchor etc., all multiplied by GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080) feeding the same pixel->NDC shader unchanged. (4) Since pixel->NDC already divides by the live ViewportSize (Frame.ViewportWidth/Height), the shader auto-maps scaled pixels to the correct NDC at any resolution. CAVEAT: if you instead wanted a single multiply, you COULD pre-multiply CBData.Transform (RmlUi.hlsl applies Transform before the NDC divide), but that only works for the RmlUi path and not the FontGeometry path, and it hides the scale from layout/hit-testing — drag/hit-test (mouse in viewport pixels) needs the scaled pixel rects anyway, so layout-stage scaling keeps render and input consistent.

evidence.note: CharW = 23.0f*Scale, CharH = 23.0f*Scale — existing 2D path multiplies geometry in PIXEL space before pixel->clip
Transform applied to pixel pos BEFORE the /ViewportSize NDC divide; CB has ViewportSize+Translation but no projection — pixel-space is the natural scaling point
RmlPerFrameCB built per draw from Frame.ViewportWidth/Height + Translation + CurrentTransform (identity by default) — no ortho matrix exists to scale
Frame carries only ViewportWidth/ViewportHeight; no GlobalScale/UIScale field exists — new field would feed layout math

openQuestions: UI pass runs BEFORE GammaCorrection (ERenderPass order), so UI colors are gamma-corrected with the scene. Confirm whether the new simple UI is authored in linear or sRGB and whether it should move after GammaCorrection — affects color, not layout/scale.

RmlUi computes its own internal layout from RmlContext->SetDimensions(viewport). A separate lightweight UI model would NOT go through RmlUi layout; it would need its own pixel-rect builder feeding the same draw path (RmlUi.hlsl or a clone). Decide whether to reuse FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11::CompileGeometry/RenderGeometry directly or add a parallel quad batcher modeled on FFontGeometry.
FUIPass::BeginPass gates on UUIManager::HasViewportWidgets(); a new system would need its own gate/has-content check and either a new ERenderPass slot or to be merged into FUIPass.

The in-game drag editor uses mouse-in-viewport pixel coords (Frame.CursorViewportX/Y already populated in GameRenderPipeline::BuildFrame, lines 203-215). Hit-testing must use the SAME GlobalScale-multiplied pixel rects used for draw — reinforces layout-stage scaling.

verdicts.claim: [B1] RmlUi D3D11 RenderInterface is the closest UI quad-batch analog: CompileGeometry builds interleaved POSITION(float2)+COLOR(float4)+TEXCOORD(float2) VB + R32 IB; RenderGeometry binds Shaders/UI/RmlUi.hlsl, NoDepth/AlphaBlend/SolidNoCull, OMSetRenderTargets(Cache.RTV/DSV), DrawIndexed; FFontGeometry::AddScreenText is a simpler screen-space quad builder (4 verts/6 idx per char).
verdicts.note: UIManager.cpp:200-262 CompileGeometry converts Rml::Vertex -> FRmlVertexD3D11 (struct at lines 31-36: float X,Y; R,G,B,A; U,V = POSITION float2 + COLOR float4 + TEXCOORD float2), creates VertexBuffer (D3D11_BIND_VERTEX_BUFFER, lines 233-245) and IndexBuffer of uint32 (lines 247-259). UIManager.cpp:264-315 RenderGeometry: GetOrCreate(UIShaderPath=='Shaders/UI/RmlUi.hlsl', confirmed line 64), SetDepthStencilState(NoDepth)/SetBlendState(AlphaBlend)/SetRasterizerState(SolidNoCull) lines 284-286, OMSetRenderTargets(1,&Ctx->Cache.RTV, Ctx->Cache.DSV) line 288, DrawIndexed line 314 with DXGI_FORMAT_R32_UINT index buffer line 313. FontGeometry.cpp:159-231 AddScreenText builds CharCount*4 verts and CharCount*6 indices (lines 175-176, 214-221). Every sub-claim verified.

verdicts.claim: [B2] Top-left origin / Y-DOWN pixel convention; pixel->NDC done in VS not via projection matrix. RmlUi.hlsl VS: x=(px/W)*2-1, y=1-(py/H)*2. FFontGeometry PixelToClipX/Y identical. FullscreenTriangleVS uses uv*float2(2,-2)+float2(-1,1). D3D viewport TopLeft 0,0 full W/H MinDepth0/MaxDepth1. CB carries ViewportSize, per-draw Translation, identity-default Transform; no ortho matrix.
verdicts.note: RmlUi.hlsl:30-33 NdcPosition = ((px/ViewportSize.x)*2-1, 1-(py/ViewportSize.y)*2) — exact top-left/Y-down formula, no projection matrix (conversion in VS). FontGeometry.cpp:187-195 PixelToClipX=(X/W)*2-1, PixelToClipY=1-(Y/H)*2 — identical. Functions.hlsli:37-38 FullscreenTriangleVS position=uv*float2(2,-2)+float2(-1,1). D3DDevice.cpp:228 ViewportInfo={0,0,W,H,0,1}. UIManager.cpp:180-187 BeginFrame sets D3D11_VIEWPORT TopLeftX/Y=0, Width/Height=Frame viewport, MinDepth0/MaxDepth1. FRmlPerFrameCB (lines 50-62) holds ViewportWidth/Height, TranslationX/Y, Transform[16] initialized to identity. Minor nuance (does not refute): VS applies Translation then mul(Transform, PixelPosition) in pixel space (lines 28-29) before NDC conversion; claim's description remains accurate.

dimensions.dimension: B3 — text/label rendering means (HP bar value text etc.)
dimensions.summary: Three distinct text facilities exist, none of which is a ready-made game-HUD screen-label API. (1) A WORLD-space billboard text path (UTextRenderComponent -> FTextRenderSceneProxy -> FFontGeometry::AddWorldText) is fully wired and actively used; it draws strings as camera-facing quads in 3D, not at screen pixels. (2) A SCREEN-space path EXISTS in the same FFontGeometry (AddScreenText) that converts a pixel position+scale to clip space and emits an OverlayFont draw — but it is effectively DEAD: its only feeder FScene::AddOverlayText has zero callers (no C++, no Lua), it is gated behind ViewModeUtils::SuppressesEditorOverlays (editor-overlay class), and its shader hardcodes a cyan color, alpha-from-red, and a fixed-grid bitmap atlas (no proportional metrics/kerning). (3) RmlUi renders its OWN text internally: UIManager loads Maplestory Bold.ttf via Rml::LoadFontFace and rasterizes glyphs in RmlUi's FreeType font engine — the engine only supplies a generic D3D11 render interface (no engine-side glyph/atlas code is shared with RmlUi). CONCLUSION for the simple model: the cleanest reuse for screen labels is the existing FFontGeometry::AddScreenText screen-text plumbing (it already does pixel->clip and a dynamic VB/IB draw), but it needs (a) a real caller hook, (b) per-label color/size parameters instead of the hardcoded cyan/23px, and (c) ideally a better font atlas, since the current FFontResource is a fixed-cell monospaced bitmap grid. The RmlUi font atlas is NOT directly reusable by the simple renderer (it lives behind RmlUi's API). If the simple UI is allowed to mount RmlUi documents, RmlUi already gives high-quality TTF labels for free; if the simple UI is a separate lightweight renderer, AddScreenText is the thing to lean on / extend.

findings.conclusion: World-space text is a complete, active path: UTextRenderComponent creates FTextRenderSceneProxy (FontBatched flag), and the collector batches CachedText into FFontGeometry::AddWorldText, which builds camera-facing billboard quads (uses CamRight/CamUp, WorldPos, WorldScale). This draws a string in 3D world space, NOT at a screen pixel position. Drawn via BuildFontCommands -> ERenderPass::Transparent with EShaderPath::Font.

evidence.note: AddWorldText builds billboard quads from WorldPos/CamRight/CamUp/WorldScale
UpdateMesh caches Text/FontScale/Font; sets FontBatched flag
AddWorldText feeds FontGeometry; BuildFontCommands emits world-text draw on Transparent pass with EShaderPath::Font

findings.conclusion: A SCREEN-space text facility DOES exist: FFontGeometry::AddScreenText(Text, ScreenX, ScreenY, ViewportW, ViewportH, Scale) maps a pixel position to clip space (PixelToClipX/Y) and emits quads. It can draw a string at a chosen screen pixel position with a Scale multiplier. So the raw capability the simple-UI labels need already exists in code.

evidence.note: AddScreenText: pixel->clip mapping, CharW/CharH = 23*Scale, builds screen quads
AddScreenText signature + ScreenVB/IB getters

findings.conclusion: The screen-text path is effectively DEAD and editor-scoped, not a game-HUD API. Its only data source FScene::AddOverlayText has ZERO callers anywhere (no C++ '.AddOverlayText(' / '->AddOverlayText(' match, no Lua match). BuildFontCommands (which emits the OverlayFont draw) early-returns under SuppressesEditorOverlays, marking it an editor-overlay debug facility. The OverlayFont pixel shader hardcodes the label color float4(0.6,1.0,1.0,col.r) (cyan) and derives alpha from the red channel only — no per-label color. AddScreenText hardcodes 23px cell size scaled by Scale and negative letter-spacing. So reusing it for HP-bar text needs new plumbing: a real caller, per-label color/size params, and removal of the editor-overlay gate.

evidence.note: AddOverlayText definition; grep found no call sites in Source or *.lua
OverlayText only fed from Scene->GetOverlayTexts(); BuildFontCommands gated by SuppressesEditorOverlays
PS hardcodes cyan color and alpha=col.r; no color input
SuppressesEditorOverlays classifies this as an editor overlay

findings.conclusion: The engine font used by both AddWorldText and AddScreenText is a FIXED-GRID BITMAP atlas, not a proportional/kerned font. FFontResource (=FTextureAtlasResource) only stores an SRV plus Columns/Rows; FFontGeometry::BuildCharInfoMap slices the atlas into uniform Columns x Rows cells (monospaced) for ASCII 32-126 then Hangul AC00-D7A3. Every glyph is the same cell size; there is no per-glyph width/advance/kerning. Good enough for numeric HP values, but lower visual quality than RmlUi's TTF text.

evidence.note: FFontResource = FTextureAtlasResource: SRV + Columns/Rows only
BuildCharInfoMap: uniform CellW=1/Columns, CellH=1/Rows monospaced grid
Font loaded from Resource.ini with Columns/Rows grid metadata

findings.conclusion: The RmlUi path renders text via RmlUi's OWN internal font engine (FreeType-based). UIManager::Initialize calls Rml::LoadFontFace on Content/Font/Maplestory Bold.ttf; RmlUi internally rasterizes glyphs and calls back into FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11 (GenerateTexture/RenderGeometry) with already-laid-out textured quads. The engine supplies NO font/glyph logic to RmlUi and gets NO reusable glyph atlas back out of it. So the simple UI cannot directly reuse RmlUi's font atlas; it would either go through RmlUi's API entirely or use the engine-side FFontGeometry atlas. If the simple model mounts an RmlUi document, it gets high-quality TTF labels for free.

evidence.note: Rml::LoadFontFace loads Maplestory Bold.ttf into RmlUi's font engine
Render interface only compiles/draws geometry and generates textures RmlUi hands it; no glyph code engine-side

findings.conclusion: A third text capability is Dear ImGui (ImGui::Text/TextUnformatted), but it is used only by editor panels (EditorMaterialInspector). It is an immediate-mode debug/editor overlay, not a game-world or hierarchy-driven HUD system, so it is not a fit for runtime HP-bar labels in the proposed Canvas->Group->Element model.

evidence.note: ImGui::Text/TextUnformatted used in editor material inspector panel only

openQuestions: Does the simple-UI design intend a SEPARATE lightweight renderer or to mount RmlUi documents? If separate, FFontGeometry::AddScreenText is the reuse target but needs per-label color/size and removal of the SuppressesEditorOverlays gate. If RmlUi, TTF labels come for free.
Which font atlas asset is registered as 'Default' in Resource.ini, and is its bitmap quality/coverage acceptable for HP/numeric labels, or is a new atlas needed?
Is the bitmap-atlas monospaced look acceptable for HUD numbers, or is proportional TTF rendering (RmlUi) required for the label aesthetic?

verdicts.note: Verified at FontGeometry.cpp:159-231. Signature matches exactly (lines 159-162). PixelToClipX/Y lambdas at 187-195 do pixel->clip mapping. CharW=CharH=23.0f*Scale at 167-168. Builds screen quads into ScreenVertices/ScreenIndices (175-230). FontGeometry.h:35-38 has the AddScreenText signature and :53-56 has GetScreenVBBuffer/GetScreenVBStride/GetScreenIBBuffer/GetScreenIndexCount getters. Raw capability genuinely exists.

FScene.cpp:383-386 defines AddOverlayText; grep across repo shows only the decl (FScene.h:55) and definition (FScene.cpp:383) — zero call sites in Source; no *.lua match for AddOverlayText/OverlayText.
