dimensions.dimension: CROSS-CUTTING: Anatomy of existing RmlUi UI system; reuse-vs-replace verdict for the new Canvas/Group/Element model
dimensions.summary: The existing UI is a fully-functional RmlUi (HTML/RCSS) layer. UUIManager (a TSingleton + FGCObject) owns a single Rml::Context "GameViewport", three custom Rml interfaces (System/File-wide/RenderInterface-D3D11), and a list of UUserWidget UObjects; each widget wraps one Rml::ElementDocument loaded from an .rml file path. It is initialized in Engine.cpp (UUIManager::Get().Initialize(Device)), driven once per frame entirely from inside the render pass FUIPass::Execute -> UUIManager::Render(Ctx) (there is NO separate game-thread tick; SetDimensions, ProcessInput, Update, Render all happen in the render pass), renders via FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11 straight to the viewport RTV, and feeds mouse/keys from InputSystem through Rml::Context::ProcessMouseMove/ButtonDown/etc. RmlUi already does full hit-testing, hover, and even native drag/dragstart/dragdrop events internally (GetElementAtPoint/GetHoverElement exist), and already has the exact "reference resolution + uniform scale" primitive the new model wants: SetDimensions(px) plus SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio(dp_ratio) — but the engine currently calls neither dp_ratio nor GetElementAtPoint. Widgets are created from Lua via UI.CreateWidget(path) and a new_usertype<UUserWidget> exposing AddToViewport/bind_click/SetText/SetProperty. VERDICT (F1): build the new simple Canvas/Group/Element model as a SEPARATE lightweight system, NOT a layer over RmlUi and NOT a replacement — coupling to RmlUi types is pervasive and total (every widget == an Rml::Document; layout/render/input all delegate to Rml), so there is essentially nothing reusable at the model level. The only thing worth reusing is the integration plumbing: the D3D11 render path pattern, the FUIPass slot, and the GameViewportClient input-capture contract (FUIInputCaptureState).

findings.checklistItem: C-init: how the existing UI is initialized
findings.conclusion: UUIManager is a TSingleton<UUIManager>+FGCObject. Engine.cpp initializes it once with the D3D11 device after the render pipeline is created, and shuts it down before LuaScriptManager (ordering matters because widgets hold sol::protected_function Lua callbacks). Initialize() news the 3 Rml interfaces, registers them, calls Rml::Initialise(), creates ONE context named "GameViewport" at 1x1, and loads a Maplestory font.

evidence.note: UUIManager::Get().Initialize(Device); after SetRenderPipeline
comment + UUIManager::Get().Shutdown(); ordered before FLuaScriptManager::Shutdown() because UI holds Lua callbacks
Initialize(): new System/File/Render interfaces, Rml::SetSystemInterface/SetFileInterface/SetRenderInterface, Rml::Initialise(), CreateContext("GameViewport",{1,1}), LoadFontFace
class UUIManager : public TSingleton<UUIManager>, public FGCObject

findings.checklistItem: C-tick/render: how/where it ticks and renders each frame
findings.conclusion: There is no independent UI tick. Everything happens inside the render pass FUIPass: BeginPass gates on Frame.ViewportRTV && HasViewportWidgets(); Execute calls UUIManager::Render(Ctx). Render() does SetDimensions(viewport px) -> ProcessInput(frame) -> RmlContext->Update() -> RenderInterface->BeginFrame -> RmlContext->Render() -> EndFrame, drawing to Ctx.Cache.RTV/DSV. So input processing, layout update and draw are all coupled to one render-thread call per frame. UI is one ERenderPass::UI slot in the pass list (after OverlayFont, before GammaCorrection).

evidence.note: BeginPass returns ViewportRTV && HasViewportWidgets(); Execute() -> UUIManager::Get().Render(Ctx)
Render(): SetDimensions, ProcessInput, FlushDeferredViewportRemovals, RmlContext->Update(), BeginFrame, RmlContext->Render(), EndFrame
ERenderPass::UI ordering entry in pass name map

findings.checklistItem: C-render-interface: how it renders to D3D11
findings.conclusion: FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11 implements Rml::RenderInterface. CompileGeometry builds D3D11 vertex/index buffers from Rml::Vertex (pos/colour/uv); RenderGeometry binds Shaders/UI/RmlUi.hlsl, sets NoDepth+AlphaBlend+SolidNoCull, OMSetRenderTargets to Ctx.Cache.RTV/DSV, uploads a per-frame CB (viewport W/H, translation, 4x4 transform) and draws indexed. Textures load via DirectXTK WICTextureLoader (LoadTexture) or raw RGBA8 (GenerateTexture); scissor uses a dedicated rasterizer state. This is a self-contained, reusable D3D11 2D-quad renderer pattern.

evidence.note: CompileGeometry: Rml::Vertex -> FRmlVertexD3D11, CreateBuffer for VB/IB
RenderGeometry: GetOrCreate(UIShaderPath), state set, OMSetRenderTargets(Ctx.Cache.RTV/DSV), per-frame CB upload, DrawIndexed
LoadTexture via DirectX::CreateWICTextureFromFileEx

findings.checklistItem: C-input/hit-testing: does RmlUi already do mouse hit-testing & drag (relevant to editor E1)
findings.conclusion: YES. UUIManager::ProcessInput feeds raw mouse position and L/R/M button down/up + wheel into Rml::Context::ProcessMouseMove/ProcessMouseButtonDown/Up/Wheel. RmlUi internally performs hit-testing, hover, focus, AND has native drag support (dragstart/drag/dragdrop events) plus GetElementAtPoint/GetHoverElement on the context. The engine code does NOT currently call GetElementAtPoint or use any drag events itself — so for the new editor's 'drag Element with mouse', if the new model is separate from RmlUi it must implement its own per-Element rect hit-test (the math FinalPos/size makes this trivial) rather than reusing Rml's. Clicks today are wired only via FWidgetClickEventListener on 'click'.

evidence.note: ProcessInput: ProcessMouseMove(MouseX,MouseY,0), ProcessMouseButtonDown/Up(0/1/2), ProcessMouseWheel; cursor from Frame.CursorViewportX/Y or InputSystem
GetHoverElement(), GetElementAtPoint(point,...) — built-in hit-testing
RegisterEventListeners adds 'click' listeners only; no drag wiring in engine code

findings.checklistItem: C-lua: how documents/widgets are created from Lua (sol)
findings.conclusion: FLuaScriptManager::RegisterUIBindings exposes new_usertype<UUserWidget>("UserWidget", bases<UObject>) with AddToViewport/AddToViewportZ/RemoveFromParent/Show/Hide/IsInViewport/bind_click/SetText/SetProperty/SetWantsMouse/WantsMouse, plus a UI table with UI.CreateWidget(path) that calls UUIManager::Get().CreateWidget(nullptr, path). So Lua drives the whole lifecycle: create widget from an .rml document path, add to viewport, bind click callbacks (sol::protected_function held in FWidgetClickEventListener). The new model would need its own analogous sol bindings (Canvas/Group/Element create + drag), but can mirror this exact pattern.

evidence.note: RegisterUIBindings: new_usertype<UUserWidget> with AddToViewport/bind_click/SetText/SetProperty/SetWantsMouse
UI.CreateWidget(DocumentPath) -> UUIManager::Get().CreateWidget(nullptr, DocumentPath)
FWidgetClickEventListener stores sol::protected_function Callback, invoked under FScopedGarbageCollectionBlocker

findings.checklistItem: D1/D2: does RmlUi already have a context-dimensions / dp-ratio concept matching RefRes1920x1080 + GlobalScale=Y/1080
findings.conclusion: YES, exactly. Rml::Context exposes SetDimensions(Vector2i)/GetDimensions() for pixel resolution AND SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio(float dp_ratio)/GetDensityIndependentPixelRatio() which is literally 'the ratio of the dp unit to the px unit' — i.e. RmlUi's built-in uniform UI scale. The required GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080 maps directly onto dp_ratio if the new model were built over RmlUi (author RCSS in dp, set dp_ratio = viewportHeight/1080). CRITICAL GAP: the engine TODAY only calls SetDimensions(viewport px) every frame in Render() and NEVER calls SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio — so currently there is zero reference-resolution/uniform-scaling behavior; RML content is laid out in raw viewport pixels. No RefRes/GlobalScale/1920x1080-as-design-res concept exists anywhere in engine code (window is merely created at 1920x1080 in WindowsApplication.cpp).

evidence.note: SetDimensions/GetDimensions and SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio/GetDensityIndependentPixelRatio (dp:px ratio = uniform scale)
Render() calls RmlContext->SetDimensions({viewportW,viewportH}) only — raw px, no dp_ratio call anywhere
window created 1920,1080 but no engine-side ReferenceResolution/GlobalScale concept

findings.conclusion: VERDICT: make the new Canvas/Group/Element model a BRAND-NEW lightweight system independent of RmlUi (option a), not a layer over it (c) and not a replacement of it (b). Justification: coupling between the existing model and RmlUi is total — UUserWidget IS a thin wrapper over Rml::ElementDocument, layout/styling is RCSS, hit-testing/hover/drag are Rml-internal, and rendering is Rml geometry. There is no clean seam to insert pivot/anchor/RectTransform math; reusing RmlUi would mean expressing the simple model as generated RML/RCSS (heavy, against the anti-overengineering mandate). The requested math (AnchorPx=ParentSize*anchor; FinalPos=ParentOrigin+AnchorPx+position-(size*pivot)) is trivial to compute directly and emit as quads. REUSE only the plumbing, not the model: (1) the FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11 pattern -> write an analogous quad renderer, (2) the FUIPass slot to draw, (3) the Lua usertype pattern. HIGHEST-COUPLING integration point = the GameViewportClient<->UIManager input-capture contract: GameViewportClient::Update calls UUIManager::Get().GetViewportInputCaptureState() every frame and uses bWantsMouse/bBlocksGame* to decide cursor visibility and game-input routing. Any new UI system MUST plug into this same contract (or the two systems will fight over the mouse). Also note both must share the single mouse stream — RmlContext consumes ProcessMouseMove/Button each frame.

evidence.note: GetViewportInputCaptureState() consumed every frame; bWantsMouse/bBlocksGameMouseLook gate game input
ApplyGameCapturePolicy: bShouldCaptureMouse depends on !UIState.bWantsMouse/!bBlocksGameInput
UUserWidget wraps a single Rml::ElementDocument* Document; all behavior delegates to Rml — nothing model-reusable
LoadDocument: RmlContext->LoadDocument(path) IS the widget — total RmlUi coupling at the model layer

openQuestions: Will the new lightweight UI and the existing RmlUi UI coexist on screen simultaneously, or is RmlUi expected to be retired once the new HUD/menus are ported? Both draw in the same ERenderPass::UI slot and both consume the same mouse stream — if coexisting, the input-capture (FUIInputCaptureState) and mouse-event ownership must be arbitrated between the two systems.

Should the new model author against RmlUi's dp_ratio (which already exists and is unused) as a quick path to RefRes scaling, or implement GlobalScale=Y/1080 in its own layout math? The latter is cleaner for a separate system but duplicates a capability RmlUi already provides.

Does the in-game editor need pixel-accurate per-Element hit-testing for drag, or could it reuse Rml::Context::GetElementAtPoint? If the new model is separate, it must implement its own rect hit-test (straightforward from the FinalPos/size math).

verdicts.claim: 1 [D1/D2: RmlUi already has context-dimensions/dp-ratio concept; SetDimensions called every frame but SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio never called; no RefRes/GlobalScale/1920x1080-design-res concept in engine; window merely created at 1920x1080]
verdicts.note: Context.h:44-47 declares SetDimensions(Vector2i)/GetDimensions(); lines 49-54 declare SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio(float dp_ratio)/GetDensityIndependentPixelRatio() with doc comment 'Changes the ratio of the dp unit to the px unit' — verbatim matches the claim. UIManager.cpp:819-822 in Render() calls RmlContext->SetDimensions({(int)ViewportWidth,(int)ViewportHeight}) using raw px only. Grep of the entire engine Source tree for SetDensityIndependentPixelRatio returns ZERO matches, confirming dp_ratio is never set. Grep for RefRes/GlobalScale/ReferenceResolution/'/1080' finds no UI-scaling concept (only two unrelated SkinnedMeshComponent.cpp 'GlobalScale' hits for mesh transforms). WindowsApplication.cpp:125 creates the window with literal 1920,1080 args to CreateWindowExW with no reference-resolution semantics. Every sub-claim is supported.

verdicts.claim: 2 [F1: option-a brand-new system verdict; total RmlUi coupling at model layer (UUserWidget wraps single Rml::ElementDocument, LoadDocument IS the widget); highest-coupling point = GameViewportClient<->UIManager input-capture contract via GetViewportInputCaptureState every frame gating mouse/game-input; shared single mouse stream consumed by RmlContext ProcessMouseMove/Button]
verdicts.note: Coupling and integration-point evidence are well-supported, but the cited function NAME is wrong. UserWidget.h:56-142 confirms UUserWidget holds a single 'Rml::ElementDocument* Document' (line 129) and all members delegate to Rml. UIManager.cpp:786-794 confirms RmlContext->LoadDocument(...) creates the Document that IS the widget (MarkDocumentLoaded). GameViewportClient.cpp:109 consumes UUIManager::Get().GetViewportInputCaptureState() and :110/:118-119 gate game mouse/keyboard on bWantsMouse/bBlocksGameMouseLook etc.; :209-213 ApplyGameCapturePolicy makes bShouldCaptureMouse depend on !bWantsMouse/!bBlocksGameInput/!bBlocksGameMouseLook — all as claimed. UIManager.cpp:862-890 confirms RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove/ProcessMouseButtonDown/Up/Wheel — single shared mouse stream, as claimed. DISCREPANCY: the claim says 'GameViewportClient::Update calls GetViewportInputCaptureState every frame' but the enclosing function at GameViewportClient.cpp:92 is ProcessInput(const FInputSystemSnapshot&, float), NOT a method named Update. The per-frame contract and gating logic are accurate; only the function name is misattributed. The architectural verdict (option a) is an opinion/recommendation, not a checkable fact, and the factual coupling premises underpinning it are confirmed.

dimensions.summary: The engine has a clean, single-window resolution pipeline that makes a once-per-resize GlobalScale = CurrentRes.Y/1080 trivial to compute. The authoritative client/back-buffer size flows: WM_SIZE -> FWindowsApplication::WndProc (WindowsApplication.cpp:66-77) -> FWindowsWindow::OnResized -> OnResizedCallback -> GEngine->OnWindowResized (EngineLoop.cpp:32-38) -> UEngine::OnWindowResized resizes the swapchain via FD3DDevice::OnResizeViewport (Engine.cpp:218-227, D3DDevice.cpp:121-137); UGameEngine::OnWindowResized additionally calls StandaloneViewport->RequestResize (GameEngine.cpp:95-106). Per frame, the offscreen FViewport's size is the single source of truth for UI: it is copied into FFrameContext.ViewportWidth/Height by SetViewportInfo (FrameContext.cpp:35-38) and the existing RmlUi UI already reads exactly these two floats to size itself (UIManager.cpp:819-822, 183-184, 293-294). So D1's best home for the fixed RefRes is FProjectSettings (existing JSON-backed singleton settings struct, ProjectSettings.h), D2's single compute point is OnWindowResized / the FViewport resize (resize-driven, not every frame), and D3 the natural application point is the UI layout pixel results (where RmlUi already consumes ViewportWidth/Height), NOT the 3D render projection. D4: there is currently NO aspect/16:9 enforcement anywhere (no WM_GETMINMAXINFO, no letterbox at window level); BlitToBackBuffer is a full-stretch fullscreen-triangle blit, so a 16:9 policy hook would be new and best lives either in the resize handler (clamp client size) or as a UI-only GlobalScale that ignores non-16:9 (simplest).

findings.conclusion: Best home for the fixed RefRes (1920x1080) constant is FProjectSettings — the existing global settings singleton. It is a TSingleton with nested option structs (FShadowOption, FPhysicsOption, FGameOption), JSON-serialized to Settings/ProjectSettings.ini (despite the .ini extension the format is JSON via SimpleJSON), and is already loaded once at startup in UGameEngine::Init. Add a small FUIOption { float RefResX=1920, RefResY=1080; } struct here (mirroring the FShadowOption pattern) rather than inventing a new config file or hardcoding in the UI manager. Alternatively, for a value that is conceptually a hard constant (16:9 only, never user-edited), a constexpr in a new UI header is acceptable — but the established engine pattern for tunable global values is FProjectSettings.

evidence.note: FProjectSettings : public TSingleton with nested FShadowOption/FPhysicsOption/FGameOption structs and SaveToFile/LoadFromFile — the canonical place for global tunables; a FUIOption with RefRes would slot in identically
FProjectSettings::Get().LoadFromFile(FProjectSettings::GetDefaultPath()) — settings loaded once during UGameEngine::Init, before viewport creation
On-disk file is actually JSON (Game/Physics/Shadow sections); confirms the serialization pattern a UI/RefRes section would follow
SaveToFile builds JSON objects per section via SimpleJSON; a RefRes section is a trivial 2-key add

findings.conclusion: The current render resolution / client size is known authoritatively in two places, and GlobalScale should be computed ONCE per resize (not every frame). (1) The window client size is captured in FWindowsWindow (Width/Height floats) and pushed on WM_SIZE. (2) The single funnel that every resize passes through is UEngine::OnWindowResized(Width,Height) — it both resizes the swapchain (FD3DDevice::OnResizeViewport) and, in the Game override, resizes the offscreen FViewport. This is the ONE pass that should compute GlobalScale = Height/1080.0f and store it (e.g. on UIManager or a UI root). Per frame, the resolution is then read indirectly via FFrameContext.ViewportWidth/Height (set from the FViewport in SetViewportInfo). Because the UI is drawn from the offscreen FViewport (whose size == client size in standalone), Height there equals CurrentRes.Y. Computing in OnWindowResized avoids a per-frame divide and matches the existing resize-driven RT recreation.

evidence.note: WM_SIZE handler: LOWORD/HIWORD(lParam) -> Window.OnResized(Width,Height) then OnResizedCallback(Width,Height) — the entry point for every resolution change
OnResizedCallback forwards to GEngine->OnWindowResized(Width,Height) — single bridge from platform to engine
UEngine::OnWindowResized: early-out on 0 size, then Renderer.GetFD3DDevice().OnResizeViewport(Width,Height). THE single resize funnel — ideal one-time GlobalScale compute site
UGameEngine::OnWindowResized calls base then StandaloneViewport->RequestResize(Width,Height); Width/Height here == client size == CurrentRes for the UI
SetViewportInfo copies FViewport GetWidth/GetHeight into Frame.ViewportWidth/ViewportHeight — the per-frame resolution the UI actually reads

findings.conclusion: The natural application point for GlobalScale is the UI LAYOUT PIXEL results, NOT the 3D render projection. Evidence: the existing RmlUi UI is resolution-driven entirely through FFrameContext.ViewportWidth/Height — UIManager::Render feeds them straight into RmlContext->SetDimensions(), and the per-draw constant buffer (FRmlPerFrameCB.ViewportWidth/Height) maps pixel coords to NDC in the UI shader. The 3D scene projection is independent: it only consumes AspectRatio (UCameraComponent::OnResize sets AspectRatio = W/H; FrameContext SetCameraInfo builds Proj from the camera). So the new simple UI model should multiply its computed layout pixels (FinalPos, size) by GlobalScale BEFORE emitting vertices/quads in viewport-pixel space — leaving the existing pixel->NDC viewport mapping untouched. Do not bake GlobalScale into the camera projection; that would (wrongly) scale the 3D world. (B-scanner also owns the pixel->NDC mapping detail.)

evidence.note: RmlContext->SetDimensions({(int)Frame.ViewportWidth,(int)Frame.ViewportHeight}) — UI layout space is driven by viewport pixels; GlobalScale would pre-multiply element sizes/positions in this same pixel space
FRmlPerFrameCB carries ViewportWidth/Height per draw; the UI shader uses these to convert pixel coords to clip space — the existing pixel->NDC contract the new model should reuse
UCameraComponent::OnResize sets only AspectRatio = W/H — the 3D projection path is separate from UI and must NOT carry GlobalScale
Default AspectRatio = 16.0f/9.0f — confirms 16:9 is already the assumed baseline for the 3D camera

findings.conclusion: Window resize is handled in FWindowsApplication::WndProc (WM_SIZE/WM_SIZING/WM_ENTERSIZEMOVE/WM_EXITSIZEMOVE). There is currently NO 16:9 / aspect enforcement of any kind: no WM_GETMINMAXINFO handler, no aspect clamp, and the final BlitToBackBuffer is a non-aspect-correcting fullscreen-triangle stretch (Draw(3,0)) of the offscreen RT onto the whole back buffer — so a non-16:9 window today simply stretches the image. Cleanest 16:9-only policy hooks, in order of simplicity for THIS engine: (a) IGNORE/UNIFORM-SCALE (simplest, matches the prompt's 'scale uniformly by GlobalScale'): compute GlobalScale = Height/1080 in OnWindowResized and accept that non-16:9 windows get pillarbox/overflow at the UI layer only — no window code changes. (b) LETTERBOX: there is already a camera-letterbox mechanism (FCameraLetterboxState + ApplyLetterboxAspect in GameRenderPipeline.cpp) that adjusts POV.AspectRatio; a UI letterbox could reuse this concept but is heavier. (c) HARD-ENFORCE 16:9 window: add a WM_GETMINMAXINFO/WM_SIZING handler in WndProc to constrain client size — most invasive. Recommendation: option (a), since the prompt explicitly wants a single uniform GlobalScale and 'no overengineering'.

evidence.note: WM_SIZE/WM_SIZING/WM_ENTERSIZEMOVE/WM_EXITSIZEMOVE handled here; NO WM_GETMINMAXINFO and no aspect clamp — confirmed by grep returning no matches for WM_GETMINMAXINFO/EnforceAspect in Platform/
BlitToBackBuffer = fullscreen-triangle Draw(3,0) stretch of the viewport SRV to the entire back buffer with no aspect/letterbox correction — current behavior stretches on non-16:9
ApplyLetterboxAspect + FCameraLetterboxState already exist as a letterbox mechanism (adjusts POV.AspectRatio) — a reusable precedent if option (b) letterbox is chosen
CreateWindowExW uses fixed 1920x1080 initial size with WS_OVERLAPPEDWINDOW (freely resizable) — window starts 16:9 but is not constrained to it

findings.conclusion: Coordinate/origin facts the B-scanner should reconcile with: (1) UI pixel space is top-left origin in viewport pixels — RmlUi (and the prompt's pivot top-left=(0,0)) agree; the offscreen FViewport is the coordinate frame, sized to client. (2) Mouse for in-game UI drag is already available in viewport-pixel space: GameRenderPipeline computes Frame.CursorViewportX/Y from InputSystem::Get().GetMouseClientPos() clamped to ViewportWidth/Height (GameRenderPipeline.cpp:203-215), and UIManager::ProcessInput consumes exactly those (UIManager.cpp:847-859) — so drag math can stay in unscaled client pixels and divide by GlobalScale to get logical UI coords. (3) The UI is rendered into the offscreen FViewport then stretch-blitted to the back buffer; since standalone FViewport size == client size, viewport pixels == back-buffer pixels (1:1) today, so no extra back-buffer-vs-viewport scale factor is needed beyond GlobalScale.

evidence.note: Frame.CursorViewportX/Y derived from GetMouseClientPos() and bounded by Frame.ViewportWidth/Height — mouse already in viewport-pixel space for UI drag
ProcessInput uses Frame.CursorViewportX/Y (fallback GetMouseClientPos) and passes to RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove — the input coordinate frame the new drag model should match
StandaloneViewport->Initialize sized to InWindow GetWidth/GetHeight — offscreen UI render target == client size, so viewport pixels == back-buffer pixels 1:1
FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11::BeginFrame sets D3D viewport to Frame.ViewportWidth/Height at TopLeft 0,0 — confirms top-left origin, full-viewport UI render

openQuestions: Should GlobalScale be stored on UUIManager (alongside RmlContext) or on a new UI-root object? UIManager is the natural cache point since it already owns SetDimensions, but the new lightweight model may want its own root distinct from the RmlUi path.

For non-16:9 windows, does the team want pillarbox/letterbox bars or simple overflow? The prompt says 'scale uniformly by Height/1080' which implies ignore-aspect (overflow), but no existing UI letterbox exists to confirm intent.

The editor path (UEditorEngine::OnWindowResized, EditorEngine.cpp:145) defers FViewport resize to the ImGui SSplitter layout, so in-editor the UI 'client size' is a panel, not the OS window. If the new UI editor runs inside an ImGui panel, GlobalScale should use the panel/viewport height, not the window height — needs confirmation of which surface the in-game editor renders into.

verdicts.claim: [D2] Window client size captured in FWindowsWindow as Width/Height floats and pushed on WM_SIZE; UEngine::OnWindowResized(Width,Height) is the single resize funnel that resizes the swapchain (FD3DDevice::OnResizeViewport) and (Game override) the offscreen FViewport; per-frame resolution read via FFrameContext.ViewportWidth/Height set in SetViewportInfo. Ideal one-time GlobalScale=Height/1080 compute site.
verdicts.note: All evidence verified. WindowsApplication.cpp:66-77 is the WM_SIZE handler: LOWORD/HIWORD(lParam) -> Window.OnResized(Width,Height) then OnResizedCallback(Width,Height). EngineLoop.cpp:32-38 forwards OnResizedCallback to GEngine->OnWindowResized. Engine.cpp:218-227 is UEngine::OnWindowResized: early-out on 0 size, then Renderer.GetFD3DDevice().OnResizeViewport(Width,Height) (the single funnel). GameEngine.cpp:95-106 calls base then StandaloneViewport->RequestResize(Width,Height). FrameContext.cpp:35-38 SetViewportInfo copies VP GetWidth/GetHeight into ViewportWidth/ViewportHeight. Cross-checked: WindowsWindow.h:18-29 stores Width/Height as floats (Width=0.f, Height=0.f) and WindowsWindow.cpp:13-17 OnResized assigns them; D3DDevice.cpp:121-130 OnResizeViewport calls SwapChain->ResizeBuffers (confirms swapchain resize). The compute-once-per-resize / GlobalScale=Height/1080 part is a forward-looking design recommendation, not a factual code claim, but is well-grounded in the verified structure.

verdicts.claim: [D3] GlobalScale should apply to UI layout pixels, not 3D projection. Existing RmlUi UI is resolution-driven via FFrameContext.ViewportWidth/Height: UIManager::Render feeds them into RmlContext->SetDimensions(); FRmlPerFrameCB.ViewportWidth/Height maps pixels to NDC in UI shader. 3D scene projection is independent, consuming only AspectRatio (UCameraComponent::OnResize sets AspectRatio=W/H; FrameContext SetCameraInfo builds Proj from camera). Do not bake GlobalScale into camera projection.
verdicts.note: All evidence verified. UIManager.cpp:819-822 calls RmlContext->SetDimensions({(int)Ctx.Frame.ViewportWidth,(int)Ctx.Frame.ViewportHeight}) in UUIManager::Render. UIManager.cpp:292-300 builds FRmlPerFrameCB CBData with ViewportWidth/Height = Ctx->Frame.ViewportWidth/Height per draw. CameraComponent.cpp:60-63 UCameraComponent::OnResize sets ONLY CameraState.AspectRatio = (float)Width/(float)Height. MinimalViewInfo.h:32 default AspectRatio = 16.0f/9.0f. Cross-checked FrameContext.cpp:8-16 SetCameraInfo: Proj = POV.CalculateProjectionMatrix(), confirming 3D projection path is built from camera POV and is independent of the UI/viewport-pixel path. The 'should multiply layout pixels by GlobalScale before emitting vertices' part is a design recommendation, not a current-code claim, but the underlying separation of UI-pixel vs 3D-projection paths is factually accurate.

dimensions.dimension: E (runtime drag editor: hit-test, drag mutation, save/load)
dimensions.summary: The engine has NO custom 2D UI hit-test: the existing RmlUi path delegates element-at-point entirely to RmlUi internally (UUIManager::ProcessInput -> RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove, UIManager.cpp:862), so the new simple model must implement its own top-most-Element-contains-point test from scratch — there is no FRect::Contains or 2D point-in-rect helper anywhere in Engine (only 3D FBoundingBox::IsContains, EngineTypes.h:112). The two reusable hit-test analogs to model on are both in the editor: (a) the gizmo axis intersection (UGizmoComponent::IntersectRayAxis / LineTraceComponent picking the nearest hit, GizmoComponent.cpp:96/353) and (b) the editor viewport picking flow FEditorViewportClient::HandleDragStart (EditorViewportClient.cpp:707) which picks gizmo-first then BVH-nearest and hands the result to FSelectionManager. For E2, the drag-mutation pattern to copy is UGizmoComponent::UpdateLinearDrag (GizmoComponent.cpp:475): first drag frame stores a reference, subsequent frames compute a delta and call a single mutation (TranslateTarget -> AddWorldOffset). The screen-px mouse delta source already exists: InputSystem::MouseDeltaX()/MouseDeltaY() (InputSystem.h:95-96) feeding UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput (GameViewportClient.cpp:92) — that delta MUST be divided by GlobalScale before being added to RectTransform.position. For E4, a save/load format IS warranted and should reuse the existing reflection-driven JSON serialization stack (FArchive/FJsonArchive over SimpleJSON, JsonArchive.h; FSceneSaveManager::SerializeProperties -> Obj->SerializeProperties(Ar, PF_Save), SceneSaveManager.cpp:451-461 and the property-iterating loader at 761-780) — i.e. make Element a reflected UObject whose RectTransform fields are PF_Save properties so the .Scene serializer auto-persists placements; the flat NodeEditor.json (id->{x,y}) is the precedent for a lightweight standalone file if a separate UI-layout asset is preferred over embedding in .Scene.

findings.conclusion: Mouse-coord -> UI-tree hit-test must be NEW code owned by the runtime UI subsystem (alongside/instead of UUIManager). Algorithm: traverse the Canvas->Group->Element tree, and using each Element's computed screen rect from the layout pass (FinalPos + size*GlobalScale), return the top-most (last-drawn / highest Z / deepest child) Element whose rect contains the mouse point. There is NO existing 2D element-at-point or point-in-rect helper to reuse — the RmlUi path hides all hit-testing inside RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove, and the only Contains-style tests in Engine are 3D (FBoundingBox::IsContains(FVector)). Model the traversal+nearest-pick structure on the gizmo picker (LineTraceComponent loops candidates, keeps best) and the selection ownership on FSelectionManager.

evidence.note: RmlUi delegates hit-test internally: ProcessInput calls RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove(MouseX,MouseY,...) — engine has no custom element-at-point logic to reuse
Only Contains-style point test is 3D FBoundingBox::IsContains(FVector)/(FBoundingBox); no 2D screen-rect Contains exists
LineTraceComponent: loop over candidate handles, track BestRayT/BestAxis, return nearest — the existing 'pick top-most/nearest of N candidates' analog to model the tree hit-test on
HandleDragStart: the canonical picking entry — gizmo-first hit test, else World->RaycastPrimitives nearest, then SelectionManager->Select(BestActor). Structural template for grab-on-click.
FSelectionManager owns SelectedActors + drives the gizmo; the 'which thing is grabbed' ownership pattern an in-game-editor UI selection should mirror

findings.conclusion: A drag must mutate ONLY RectTransform.position (anchor & pivot stay fixed). The mutation owner should be the runtime UI subsystem's per-frame update, modeled on UGizmoComponent::UpdateLinearDrag: on the first drag frame store the grab reference, then each frame take the mouse delta and add it to position. CRITICAL: the engine's mouse delta is in screen pixels (InputSystem::MouseDeltaX()/MouseDeltaY()), and because FinalPos = ParentOrigin + ParentSize*anchor + position - size*pivot is later multiplied by GlobalScale (=CurrentRes.Y/1080) when computing the screen rect, the screen-px delta MUST be divided by GlobalScale before adding to position: position += FVector2(MouseDeltaX, MouseDeltaY) / GlobalScale. The input enters at UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput (the runtime, not the editor, viewport client).

evidence.note: UpdateLinearDrag: bIsFirstFrameOfDrag stores LastIntersectionLocation, then FullDelta = Current - Last -> DragAmount -> HandleDrag. The 'store ref then apply per-frame delta' drag pattern to copy.
TranslateTarget: single mutation site Target->AddWorldOffset(Delta) — analog of mutating only RectTransform.position
MouseDeltaX()/MouseDeltaY() return per-frame mouse delta in SCREEN PIXELS (FrameMouseDeltaX/Y) — must be divided by GlobalScale before adding to position
UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput(FInputSystemSnapshot,...) is the runtime input owner where an in-game drag editor hooks; Snapshot carries MouseDeltaX/MouseDeltaY (lines 49-50)

findings.conclusion: YES, a save/load format for placements is needed (drag results are runtime mutations to RectTransform.position that must persist). Reuse the existing reflection-driven JSON serialization stack rather than inventing a new one: FArchive/FJsonArchive (over SimpleJSON/json.hpp) already serializes float arrays, FVector, FRotator, object references, and nested objects. FSceneSaveManager serializes the actor/component tree by calling Obj->SerializeProperties(Ar, PF_Save) and reloads by iterating reflected FProperty list and calling Property->Serialize. Recommendation: make Element/RectTransform a reflected type whose pivot/anchor/position/size are PF_Save properties (note: serializer currently has FVector/FVector4 but NO Vec2 path — SerializeVector2 must be added, ~3 lines mirroring SerializeVector). Then placements persist for free via the .Scene save path. The flat NodeEditor.json (node-id -> {x,y} map + selection + view) is the precedent if a separate lightweight UI-layout asset file is preferred over embedding in .Scene; reuse the same SimpleJSON lib either way. Do NOT add a third serialization mechanism.

evidence.note: SerializeProperties -> Obj->SerializeProperties(Ar, PF_Save) via FSceneJsonSaveArchive; reflection-driven, auto-serializes any reflected PF_Save property
Loader iterates reflected FProperty list, skips non-PF_Save, calls Property->Serialize(Obj, Ar) then fires PostEditChangeProperty — the auto-deserialize path a reflected RectTransform would ride
FJsonArchive already serializes float/string/bool/FVector(3)/FVector4/FRotator/object-ref; SerializeVector (160-163) handles 3-component only — no 2-component Vec2 helper yet (would need adding)
Existing lightweight standalone layout file: flat {node-id -> {location:{x,y}}} map + selection + view block — precedent/template for a per-canvas UI-placement json if not embedding in .Scene
.Scene format entry points SaveSceneAsJSON/LoadSceneFromJSON; SceneExtension = .Scene — the canonical reuse target for persisting drag placements with the rest of the scene

findings.conclusion: End-to-end runtime drag flow to assemble from existing patterns: (1) on LBUTTON down, run the new tree hit-test (E1) to set GrabbedElement and mark first-frame-of-drag; (2) while LeftDragging, read InputSystem MouseDeltaX/Y, divide by GlobalScale, add to GrabbedElement->RectTransform.position, re-run layout pass; (3) on LBUTTON up, clear GrabbedElement. This mirrors the gizmo state machine: down->HandleDragStart sets pressed; dragging->SetHolding(true) then UpdateDrag; up->DragEnd resets first-frame flag (EditorViewportClient.cpp:609-693, GizmoComponent.cpp:564-579). Note the editor path uses 3D rays via DeprojectScreenToWorld; the UI path is purely 2D screen-space so it is simpler (no ray, just point-in-rect). The drag state (bIsFirstFrameOfDrag, GrabbedElement) should live on the runtime UI subsystem, analogous to FEditorViewportClient holding bIsMarqueeSelecting / Gizmo holding state.

evidence.note: Full down/drag/up state machine: GetKeyDown(VK_LBUTTON)->HandleDragStart, GetLeftDragging()->SetHolding+UpdateDrag, GetLeftDragEnd()->DragEnd — the drag lifecycle template
UpdateDrag/DragEnd: bIsFirstFrameOfDrag reset on DragEnd — the per-drag-session state reset pattern the UI drag should replicate
Editor converts screen mouse to local viewport coords then DeprojectScreenToWorld (3D). The UI model skips the ray entirely — work in 2D screen space, so simpler than this path.

openQuestions: Where should the runtime UI subsystem's drag state and per-frame hit-test/update live — extend UUIManager (currently RmlUi-only, no per-Element model) or add a new parallel subsystem? UUIManager has no Element tree, so a new lightweight system is implied; confirm against Engine.cpp init ordering.

Should placements embed in the existing .Scene file (Element as reflected UObject in the world) or live in a separate per-Canvas json asset like NodeEditor.json? Depends on whether Canvas/Element are world actors or standalone UI assets — not resolvable from Dimension E alone.

FJsonArchive/SceneSaveManager have no 2-component Vec2 serialization path (only FVector/FVector4); a SerializeVector2 must be added to reuse the reflection serializer for RectTransform — confirm FProperty/reflection registers a Vec2 type at all (Dimension on reflection/types should verify).

verdicts.claim: [E1] Mouse->UI-tree hit-test must be NEW code; no existing 2D element-at-point/point-in-rect helper to reuse; RmlUi hides hit-test in RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove; only Contains tests are 3D FBoundingBox; model traversal on gizmo LineTraceComponent and selection ownership on FSelectionManager.
verdicts.note: All five citations open and support the claim. UIManager.cpp:861-862 is verbatim RmlContext->ProcessMouseMove(MouseX,MouseY,KeyModifierState) — RmlUi owns hit-testing, no custom element-at-point in engine (UI dir is RmlUi-only: UIManager + UserWidget). EngineTypes.h:105-117 shows ONLY 3D Contains: IsContains(FBoundingBox) at 105-110 and IsContains(FVector) at 112-117; no 2D screen-rect Contains anywhere (grep for RectTransform/anchor/pivot/FinalPos finds no UI layout system). GizmoComponent.cpp:353-410 LineTraceComponent loops axes, tracks BestRayT/BestAxis, returns nearest — accurate analog. EditorViewportClient.cpp:707-758 HandleDragStart does gizmo-first (RaycastComponent), else World->RaycastPrimitives, then SelectionManager->Select(BestActor) — accurate. SelectionManager.h:20-55 FSelectionManager owns SelectedActors (line 51) and drives gizmo (GetGizmo/SyncGizmo) — accurate. The Canvas->Group->Element tree / screen-rect formula are prescriptive (NEW design), consistent with the claim's own 'NEW code' framing, not asserted as existing.

verdicts.claim: [E2] Drag mutates only RectTransform.position; owner is runtime per-frame update modeled on UGizmoComponent::UpdateLinearDrag (store grab ref first frame, then per-frame delta); engine mouse delta is screen px (InputSystem::MouseDeltaX/Y), and because FinalPos=ParentOrigin+ParentSize*anchor+position-size*pivot is multiplied by GlobalScale(=CurrentRes.Y/1080), screen-px delta must be divided by GlobalScale; input enters at UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput.
verdicts.note: The CITED code evidence is all accurate. GizmoComponent.cpp:475-502 UpdateLinearDrag: bIsFirstFrameOfDrag stores LastIntersectionLocation (490) then FullDelta=Current-Last (495)->DragAmount->HandleDrag — exactly the store-ref-then-per-frame-delta pattern. GizmoComponent.cpp:318-328 TranslateTarget single AddWorldOffset mutation — accurate. InputSystem.h:95-96 MouseDeltaX()/MouseDeltaY() return FrameMouseDeltaX/Y in screen pixels — accurate. GameViewportClient.cpp:92 is verbatim UGameViewportClient::ProcessInput(const FInputSystemSnapshot&, float). HOWEVER two embedded factual assertions are NOT grounded in existing code and read as existing facts: (1) FinalPos=...*GlobalScale, GlobalScale=CurrentRes.Y/1080, and RectTransform.position do NOT exist anywhere in the codebase (no RectTransform/anchor/pivot/FinalPos/CurrentRes in any UI source; CurrentRes/1080 only appear unrelated at EditorViewportClient.h:131 WindowHeight=1080 and WindowsApplication.cpp:125). These are part of the proposed NEW UI layout model, not a verified engine fact. (2) Minor citation slip: the claim cites Snapshot MouseDeltaX/MouseDeltaY at GameViewportClient.cpp lines 49-50, but those lines are reset-to-zero statements (Snapshot.MouseDeltaX=0); the struct fields actually live in InputSystem.h:19-20. The field-carries claim is true, citation location is imprecise.
verdicts.correctedEvidence: FInputSystemSnapshot fields MouseDeltaX/MouseDeltaY declared at KraftonEngine/Source/Engine/Input/InputSystem.h:19-20 (GameViewportClient.cpp:49-50 are zero-out resets, not the declaration). The FinalPos/GlobalScale(=CurrentRes.Y/1080)/RectTransform.position formula has NO existing-code basis (grep finds no RectTransform/anchor/pivot/FinalPos/CurrentRes UI layout); it is prescriptive NEW design, not a cited engine fact.

dimensions.dimension: Graphics API surface / rendering primitive available for the simple UI
dimensions.summary: The trigger prompt's claim of "DirectX/Vulkan direct calls" is half right and half wrong. The renderer is Direct3D 11 ONLY — there is no Vulkan and no D3D12 anywhere in KraftonEngine/Source (zero matches for `\bVulkan\b`, `\bvk[A-Z]`, `\bD3D12\b`, `D3D12CreateDevice`, `ID3D12Device`). The device is created via a single `D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain` call pinned to `D3D_FEATURE_LEVEL_11_0`. There is NO RHI/abstraction layer: D3D calls are made directly against raw `ID3D11Device`/`ID3D11DeviceContext` COM interfaces throughout. The device, immediate context, and swapchain are all owned by the value-type `FD3DDevice` (member of `FRenderer`, which is a value member of `UEngine`, reachable globally via `extern UEngine* GEngine`). A UI quad renderer would get its handle via `GEngine->GetRenderer().GetFD3DDevice().GetDeviceContext()` (or `.GetDevice()`), or — during a render pass — via the already-passed `FPassContext::Device`. The actual rendering primitive surface is therefore raw immediate-mode D3D11: bind shader, set vertex/index buffers + constant buffer, `Draw`/`DrawIndexed`. The existing RmlUi path (`UIManager.cpp`, class `FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11`) already proves this exact pattern for screen-space UI, so the simple UI can reuse the same primitive surface without inventing any new device plumbing.

findings.checklistItem: API: DirectX-only vs DirectX+Vulkan; D3D version
findings.conclusion: Renderer is Direct3D 11 ONLY — no Vulkan, no D3D12. Device is created with D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain pinned to D3D_FEATURE_LEVEL_11_0. The design doc can safely treat the engine as a single-backend D3D11 renderer; the prompt's 'Vulkan' and unqualified 'DirectX' framing is incorrect.

evidence.note: featureLevels[] = { D3D_FEATURE_LEVEL_11_0 } — D3D11, single feature level
D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain(...) with D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE, D3D11_SDK_VERSION — the one and only device creation call
Members are ID3D11Device*, ID3D11DeviceContext*, IDXGISwapChain* — D3D11/DXGI types
#include <d3d11sdklayers.h> — D3D11 debug layer header; no Vulkan/D3D12 headers anywhere

findings.checklistItem: RHI abstraction vs direct D3D calls
findings.conclusion: No RHI abstraction layer. Calls go directly against raw ID3D11DeviceContext COM methods (OMSetRenderTargets, RSSetViewports, PSSetShaderResources, Draw, ClearRenderTargetView, etc.). FD3DDevice is a thin owner, not an abstraction. The simple UI should likewise issue raw D3D11 immediate-context calls rather than expect a portable RHI.

evidence.note: BeginFrame() calls DeviceContext->ClearRenderTargetView / RSSetViewports / OMSetRenderTargets directly
BlitToBackBuffer: raw Ctx->PSSetShaderResources / IASetPrimitiveTopology / Ctx->Draw(3,0) — a full-screen-triangle blit, the closest existing analog to a UI quad draw
BlitToBackBuffer(ID3D11ShaderResourceView*) — public API takes raw D3D11 SRV; confirms no abstraction over resource handles

findings.checklistItem: Where device/context/swapchain are owned and accessed (handle a UI quad renderer needs)
findings.conclusion: FD3DDevice owns Device/Context/SwapChain. It is a value member of FRenderer (Renderer.h:52), which is a value member of UEngine (Engine.h:88), reachable globally via `extern UEngine* GEngine` (Engine.h:94). A UI quad renderer obtains its context via GEngine->GetRenderer().GetFD3DDevice().GetDeviceContext(), or inside a render pass via FPassContext::Device. Accessors: FD3DDevice::GetDevice()/GetDeviceContext() (D3DDevice.h:23-24); FRenderer::GetFD3DDevice() (Renderer.h:30); UEngine::GetRenderer() (Engine.h:63).

evidence.note: GetDevice() / GetDeviceContext() public accessors — the handle entry points
FRenderer::GetFD3DDevice() returns FD3DDevice& (Renderer owns Device at line 52)
UEngine::GetRenderer() returns FRenderer&; member 'FRenderer Renderer' at line 88
extern UEngine* GEngine — global handle giving any UI code reach to the device

findings.checklistItem: Existing screen-space UI render pattern to reuse (RmlUi path proof)
findings.conclusion: The existing RmlUi UI already implements screen-space rendering by pulling the same D3D11 immediate context via Ctx->Device.GetDeviceContext() and issuing raw draws (CompileGeometry builds vertex/index buffers; sets viewport/scissor; draws). This is the proven template for the new simple UI's quad renderer — it confirms the rendering primitive surface (raw ID3D11DeviceContext draw with a per-frame constant buffer) and that screen-space UI is fed via FPassContext during the pass pipeline.

evidence.note: FRmlRenderInterfaceD3D11::BeginFrame: ID3D11DeviceContext* DC = Ctx->Device.GetDeviceContext(); then RSSetViewports — UI grabs context from FPassContext
Ctx->Device.GetDeviceContext()->RSSetScissorRects(...) — scissor-clipped UI draw via raw context (relevant for hierarchical clip)
CompileGeometry builds FRmlVertexD3D11 vertex/index buffers — existing screen-space vertex pipeline the simple UI could mirror or share

openQuestions: FPassContext.Device is a reference/value of FD3DDevice passed into passes (UIManager uses Ctx->Device.GetDeviceContext()); confirm whether the new simple UI should render as a dedicated FRenderPass inside the pass pipeline (like RmlUi) or as a separate post-present overlay — both have access to the same single immediate context.

The simple UI's quad shader would need an entry in EShaderPath / FShaderManager (same registry BlitToBackBuffer uses via EShaderPath::Blit). Not verified here whether a suitable 2D/UI vertex shader already exists or must be added.

verdicts.claim: Renderer is Direct3D 11 ONLY — no Vulkan, no D3D12. Device created via D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain pinned to D3D_FEATURE_LEVEL_11_0; single-backend D3D11 renderer.
verdicts.note: D3DDevice.cpp:151 has featureLevels[] = { D3D_FEATURE_LEVEL_11_0 } (single level). D3DDevice.cpp:191-193 is the lone D3D11CreateDeviceAndSwapChain call with D3D_DRIVER_TYPE_HARDWARE and D3D11_SDK_VERSION. D3DDevice.h:37-39 members are ID3D11Device*, ID3D11DeviceContext*, IDXGISwapChain*. D3DDevice.h:4 includes <d3d11sdklayers.h>. A targeted grep for vulkan/d3d12/d3dx12/VkDevice/directx12 across Source returned no matches (the only case-insensitive 'dxc'-substring hits are unrelated false positives). Single-backend D3D11 framing is correct.

verdicts.claim: FD3DDevice owns Device/Context/SwapChain; value member of FRenderer (Renderer.h:52), value member of UEngine (Engine.h:88), reachable via extern UEngine* GEngine (Engine.h:94). Accessors: FD3DDevice::GetDevice()/GetDeviceContext() (D3DDevice.h:23-24); FRenderer::GetFD3DDevice() (Renderer.h:30); UEngine::GetRenderer() (Engine.h:63). Inside a render pass via FPassContext::Device.
verdicts.note: D3DDevice.h:23-24 declares public GetDevice()/GetDeviceContext(); members Device/DeviceContext/SwapChain at lines 37-39. Renderer.h:30 GetFD3DDevice() returns FD3DDevice&, owning 'FD3DDevice Device' at line 52. Engine.h:63 GetRenderer() returns FRenderer&, owning 'FRenderer Renderer' at line 88.
